title: RapidStream SLO qualification & cost levers — follow-up
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alejandro Torres <alejandro@rapidstream.ai>
Cc: Priya Shah <pshah@rapidstream.ai>
Date: Sun, 09 May 2027 15:07:00 -0700
Subject: RapidStream discovery recap — latency & streaming targets + next steps

Alejandro — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment before looping in SE/CS: 

Summary of what you asked for (please correct if I’m off):
- SLO targets: p95 inference latency <= 300 ms for typical prompt lengths (you mentioned 512–768 tokens in the longest flows). p50 target ideally < 120 ms.
- Streaming: UI requires partial-token streaming; first byte/time-to-first-token should be < 150 ms for most requests.
- Concurrency/throughput: steady concurrent requests ~150–200 RPS; peak bursts up to ~350 for ~1–3 minutes during rapid user surges.
- Retention & security: no persistent user PII storage, VPC option preferred for the POC.
- Timeline: 6-week proof-of-concept, then scale to production if metrics look good.

Attached I’ve added a simple SLO matrix and a one-page architecture sketch. Next steps I propose:
1) You confirm average/peak token distribution (avg token per request, % of requests >512 tokens).
2) SE (Karthik) to propose an initial config: concurrency pools, batch windows, caching strategy, and cost estimate.
3) Align on sampling/eval prompt set that we’ll use to track quality vs cheaper model variants.

If that reads right, I’ll queue Karthik and Marissa to scope the POC and draft an initial commercial outline.

Best,
Ben
Account Executive | Redwood Inference


From: Alejandro Torres <alejandro@rapidstream.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Shah <pshah@rapidstream.ai>
Date: Sun, 09 May 2027 18:33:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RapidStream discovery recap — latency & streaming targets + next steps

Ben — this is great, thanks. A couple clarifications from our side: 
- Token distribution: mean ~120 tokens/request; roughly 10% of calls exceed 512 tokens and ~2% exceed 768.
- Concurrency: our normal steady-state is ~160 RPS; peak usually hits ~220 RPS and lasts 2–5 minutes. The 350 estimate was an upper-bound for holiday/marketing spikes (we may not need to design for that in the POC).
- Streaming: first-byte < 120 ms is preferred; client can tolerate small inter-token jitter but UX is very sensitive to initial responsiveness.
- Data: we cannot persist user chat logs outside our environment; any KV/prefix caching must be customer-controlled (token-level ephemeral cache or VPC-hosted cache).

Procurement: we’d like a 6-week POC with an SLA-ish commitment around uptime during the test and a defined rollback/cost cap. I’ll introduce our procurement contact once you have a POC draft.

Happy to kick the POC kickoff next Tuesday if you’re ready.

—Alejandro
CTO, RapidStream


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alejandro Torres <alejandro@rapidstream.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Priya Shah <pshah@rapidstream.ai>
Date: Mon, 10 May 2027 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RapidStream discovery recap — latency & streaming targets + next steps

Appreciate the details, Alejandro — that distribution helps a lot. Karthik / Marissa — looping you both in to propose an initial technical and commercial approach. 

High-level cost/perf levers I recommend we evaluate in the POC (tradeoffs noted):
- Continuous batching with tight batch windows (10–25ms): improves throughput and lowers per-token cost but can add ~10–40 ms to p50 depending on load shape. We can tune aggressively for p95 SLOs.
- Client-side prefix/KV caching (customer-managed): if you can expose repetitive prompts (top 500 prompt signatures), we can reduce compute by ~30% on those flows with zero Redwood retention. This matches your no-persist requirement if cache runs in your VPC.
- Quantization profiles: q4f16 provides ~2–2.5x token cost reduction vs fp16 with modest quality delta on instruction-following tasks; q8* options are a middle ground. We should run your eval set to measure quality delta.
- Model variants & fallback routing: primary model = redwood-large-v1 for quality; fallback = redwood-medium-v1 or an open 13B quantized variant for heavy bursts. Canary routing keeps UX impact minimal.
- Streaming tuning: enable immediate-first-token streaming path (bypass some batching for the first tokens) + continuous KV cache to accelerate follow-ups.
- Autoscale & concurrency pools: reserve a dedicated pool for streaming-critical endpoints to avoid head-of-line blocking by batch-oriented paths.

Ask from you: share a small labeled eval set (20–50 representative prompts + desired outputs) and a sample token histogram so Karthik can simulate realistic batch profiles. I’ll also add cost ballpark numbers in the POC doc — expected delta across levers.

If we’re aligned, I’ll schedule a kickoff (Karthik/Marissa) Tue 10:30 PT and draft a POC SOW + cost sheet.

Ben
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Attachments: RS-SLO-matrix-20270509.xlsx, redwood-arch-quickview.pdf


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Alejandro Torres <alejandro@rapidstream.ai>
Date: Mon, 10 May 2027 11:44:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: RapidStream discovery recap — technical knobs and initial estimates

Thanks — short technical note and what I’ll bring to the kickoff: 

Initial perf estimates (ballpark for your distribution):
- redwood-large-v1 (fp16)
  - p50: ~95–130 ms (single-token hot-path streaming), p95: ~260–320 ms depending on batching settings and token length.
- redwood-large-v1 (q4f16)
  - p50: ~110–150 ms, p95: ~220–280 ms — cost ~40–55% of fp16.
- redwood-medium-v1 (q4) fallback
  - p50: ~70–100 ms, p95: ~160–220 ms — lower quality on complex synth tasks.

Config suggestions for POC:
- Batch window: 12 ms default, dynamically widen to 25 ms under high load; implement first-token bypass for streaming endpoints.
- KV/prefix cache: customer-hosted Redis in VPC; Redwood runtime exposes read-only cache hooks; expect 20–35% hit rate for repetitive prompts based on similar workloads.
- Concurrency pools: 2 pools — streaming-critical (hot) sized for 250 RPS headroom; batch-optimized pool for bulk traffic.
- Fallback routing: routes by latency headroom and cost tier — automatic fallback if p95 > SLO for N consecutive 10s windows.
- Quantization profile test matrix: fp16 / q8_0 / q4f16 across your eval set; we’ll report quality-impact metrics (BLEU/ROUGE-ish, human pass rate).

I’ll prepare a short sim that estimates token-seconds and projected spend under each configuration; will bring that to the kickoff plus a one-sheet with the exact POC acceptance criteria.

Karthik
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference


From: Alejandro Torres <alejandro@rapidstream.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 May 2027 09:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RapidStream SLO qualification & cost levers — follow-up

All clear — thanks, team. We’re good to kickoff Tue 11:30 PT (can you confirm calendar invite). I’ll have procurement on standby — include a simple statement of work and a proposed 6-week POC commercial that includes an agreed cost cap for the test. Also please include a brief non-persist/data handling note for our legal team.

Docs to send ahead: NDA (if needed), POC SOW, a short evaluation rubric, and the cost impact sheet Karthik mentioned.

We’ll share the small eval set and the token histogram by end of day today. Looking forward to the kickoff.

—Alejandro
CTO, RapidStream
Attachments referenced: RapidStream-POC-NDA.docx (to be emailed by procurement)

title: Private-offer pricing check: unit economics & marketplace path
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mike Jensen <mike.jensen@acornhealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-08T15:12:00-07:00
Subject: Private-offer pricing check: unit economics & marketplace path

Hi Mike — thanks for taking the call earlier. Quick recap and a few clarifying asks so we can line up the private-offer flow for procurement.

Recap from discovery:
- Expected model surface: Llama-3-Chat / 8k context for patient summarization flows
- Estimated steady-state token mix: ~80% input / 20% output
- Target monthly volumes (baseline): 5M — 12M tokens/month (burstable to 20M)
- Procurement preference: AWS Marketplace private offer (recommended) — procurement@acornhealth indicated they'd prefer marketplace billing
- Target economics (from your comments): looking for a target in the ~$700–$950 per 1M tokens range depending on volume and quantization profile

Questions / asks:
1) Can you confirm the expected average monthly token volume (pick one): 5M / 10M / 15M ?
2) Is the procurement path confirmed as AWS Marketplace private offer? (or will you use GCP Marketplace / direct PO)
3) Budget cadence: do you expect a monthly budget allocation or a quarterly/annual committed amount?

What I'll do next:
- Attach a preliminary private-offer mapping (Redwood_Private_Offer_Mapping.xlsx) that maps our internal SKU -> AWS Marketplace SKUs and shows how unit pricing flows into a private-offer line item.
- Share a short pricing matrix with three tiered unit-price scenarios so your procurement team can see committed vs on-demand economics.

Attachments: Redwood_Private_Offer_Mapping.xlsx (XLSX, 120KB), Pricing_Matrix_v1.pdf (PDF, 85KB)

Thanks — if you reply with the three items above I can get Sales Ops & Partner team engaged and draft the private offer for review.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter | Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0183

---
Redwood Inference — making inference predictable and economical for engineering teams


From: Mike Jensen <mike.jensen@acornhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@acornhealth.com
Date: 2027-05-09T09:36:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Private-offer pricing check: unit economics & marketplace path

Ben — thanks for the summary and the quick turnaround. A few answers below: 

1) Volume: leaning toward the 10M tokens/month baseline for Q3-PoC, rising to ~18M by Q1 2028 if performance is good.
2) Procurement path: yes — AWS Marketplace private offer is preferred (our finance team wants marketplace billing for vendor consolidation).
3) Budget cadence: monthly commit for the PoC (we can convert to quarterly/annual for committed reserve later).

A couple of asks from procurement side: 
- Can you provide sample PO language we can drop into the private-offer request?
- We need to see the net unit price that will appear on the marketplace invoice (i.e., the PPU that Redwood will accept when the private offer is created). Are the tiered rates you mentioned the final marketplace-visible prices or do they require a separate mapping?

I'll loop in procurement@acornhealth.com for formal PO steps — they asked if you can also confirm whether any reserved-capacity commitment is required on our side to get the lowest PPU band.

Best,
Mike Jensen
Director of Product (AI) — Acorn Health
mike.jensen@acornhealth.com | +1 (617) 555-4321


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mike Jensen <mike.jensen@acornhealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-10T11:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Private-offer pricing check: unit economics & marketplace path

Mike — perfect, thanks for confirming. Answering procurement asks and next steps below.

Marketplace-visible unit price and tiers (illustrative — final will be set in the private offer document):
- Tier A (0–1M): $1,200 per 1M tokens (on-demand)
- Tier B (1M–10M): $950 per 1M tokens
- Tier C (>10M): $800 per 1M tokens

Target negotiated pricing (what we can aim to put into the private offer for your 10–18M profile):
- 10M baseline: $900 per 1M tokens
- 15M committed: $820 per 1M tokens
- >20M: custom reserved pricing (would require a 6–12 month commitment)

Marketplace notes:
- The AWS Marketplace private offer will show the SKU and the unit price we put in the offer. The invoice will reflect the private-offer unit price (i.e., the Tier/negotiated PPU). No separate hidden mapping is required — we map our internal SKU to the Marketplace SKU and the private offer sets the actual PPU.
- Reserved capacity on Redwood Dedicated is optional for the PoC — not required to create the private offer. Reserved capacity is only required if you want guaranteed latency SLA and dedicated GPU pools. We can keep this as hosted inference behind the marketplace private offer while you validate.

Attached is an updated Pricing_Matrix_v1.pdf with a column that shows the marketplace-visible SKU/PPU mapping and a sample PO language snippet you can paste into the private-offer request: 

Sample PO line (suggested):
- Product: Redwood Inference — Hosted LLM Inference (Redwood SKU: RW-HOST-CHAT-8K)
- Units: per 1M tokens
- Unit Price: $900 per 1M tokens (as negotiated in AWS Marketplace private offer #TBD)
- Term: monthly billing; initial PoC term 3 months

Next steps (what we'll do):
1) Marissa (cc) will open a Partner ticket to draft the AWS Marketplace private offer and create the marketplace SKU mapping.
2) Laura will prepare a one-page SOW and sample PO language for your procurement team to attach to the private offer request.
3) Ben (me) will prepare a short invoice example and HubSpot deal update.

Marissa / Laura — can you confirm ETA for the draft private offer and the SOW? Marissa, please include the AWS SKU we should use and whether we need any additional details from Acorn procurement.

Thanks — will stand by for procurement email/blog.

Best,
Ben

Attachments: Pricing_Matrix_v1.pdf (updated), Acorn_MA-questions.docx (procurement checklist)


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-11T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Private-offer pricing check: unit economics & marketplace path

Thanks Ben — I'll take this. Marissa, I'll open the Partner ticket now and request the private-offer draft.

Action items I will own:
- Open Partner ticket and request private-offer draft + confirm Marketplace SKU mapping (ETA: 48 hours)
- Produce a one-page SOW and PO snippet for Acorn procurement to paste into the AWS private-offer form (ETA: 24 hours)
- Add HubSpot note on deal ACORN-2027-044 with proposed PPU bands and expected volume profile

Marissa — if you need anything from Acorn procurement, I'll ask Ben to get that over.

Laura Bennett | Sales Operations Manager
laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-11T09:00:00Z
Subject: Approval request: pilot pricing + SLA/security exceptions for ClearPath (formal ask)

Lauren / Marissa — asking for formal approval to proceed with the following concessions for ClearPath pilot: 

Proposal (what we will offer to ClearPath for a 3-month pilot):
- Commercial: 20% pilot discount from list for pilot usage (pilot cap $12k).
- SLA: pilot-only SLA variance to 99.5% with non-recurring financial credits capped at 10% of pilot fees.
- Security: provide SOC 2 artifacts + 1-page security memo; require customer to use SFTP push with TTL on raw payloads (30 days).

Business justification:
- ClearPath is a mid-size regional health network with credible payer relationships; winning this pilot opens a path to enterprise rollout across 12 hospitals. Tom Chen is the economic buyer and is prepared to run a pilot quickly if procurement timeline is reasonable.
- Competitive position: they are evaluating three vendors; our combination of performance and observability is a differentiator.

Risk & mitigations:
- Risk: SLA credits and discount reduce short-term revenue. Mitigation: cap pilot to $12k and credits limited to 10% of pilot fees; require signed MOU before work begins.
- Risk: data residency/compliance. Mitigation: require SFTP with TTL + SOC 2 evidence; security memo will be explicit.

Requested approvals:
1) Lauren — approve the 20% pilot discount and MOU approach.
2) Marissa — confirm we can file this as a standard pilot SOW and route to Finance for accounting of the one-time discount.
3) Dev — please confirm you’ll produce the 1-page security memo within 48 hours of getting the procurement questionnaire.

If you approve, I will send a formal MOU with the terms and the mutual action plan to Sarah and Tom and attach the SLA draft.

Ben
Sales Lead | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
Attachment: SLA_draft_ClearPath_99_5.pdf

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Rajiv Singh <rajiv@horizonpay.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: HorizonPay renewal & embedding capacity proposal

Hi Rajiv —

Hope all is well. HorizonPay’s annual commit is up on June 30th; we pulled together a renewal package that addresses the three things you mentioned on our last call: a 12→18 month price-hold window, a modest one-time credit to smooth the Q3 true-up, and an optional add-on for embeddings capacity (prepaid blocks) plus an estimate for Dedicated capacity if you want a predictable tail-latency SLA.

Attached are: 1) Q1 usage report, 2) a sizing workbook for embeddings and Dedicated, and 3) our formal offer PDF. High-level summary:
- Renewal term: 12 months (baseline) or 18 months for a deeper effective discount
- Price-hold: 9 months guaranteed at current committed per-token rate; month 10–18 follows a capped step schedule
- One-time credit: $75k to apply to Q3 true-up if signed by May 31
- Embeddings: prepaid blocks (100M vectors) with preferred unit pricing; SES + SRE scoping included
- Dedicated: estimated 2x A100 equivalent pool for peak hour — pricing in workbook

Can you review and let me know which path you prefer (12 vs 18 months) and whether you want us to include the Dedicated line in the main SOW or as a separate add-on? If you want, I can route the 18-month pricing to Aditya for approval (we’ll need VP sign-off for anything >15% net concession).

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0127

Attachments: horizonpay_usage_report_q1.pdf (pdf), embedding-sizing.xlsx (xlsx), redwood_offer_HP-2027.pdf (pdf)

Ben —

Thanks for the package and the fast turnaround. A few quick notes from our side:
1) We prefer an 18-month window if the economics are better, but we need a binding price-hold for at least the first 12 months.
2) The $75k credit is helpful; can that be applied across billing months (three-way split) rather than one lump redemption?
3) Embeddings: our product team wants a starter block (100M) with the option to top-up monthly and a guaranteed throughput SLA for embed inference.
4) Dedicated: we’re interested but procurement will likely request a separate SOW and an estimated capex/burn model, not mixed into the main renewal.

Also — procurement@horizonpay.com needs a T&E sheet and signed offer (PDF) to start internal approvals. Can you confirm the DocuSign flow?

Maya and I are available for a 30-minute call on Thursday to talk the numbers.

title: Renewal & capacity uplift — paperwork and MSA redline coordination
preview:
From: Sam Torres <sam.torres@deeplytics.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-04T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Renewal + 60% capacity increase — next steps for paperwork

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We ran the numbers and want to proceed with the renewal + a 60% capacity increase across our inference pods starting July 1. Attached is the order form Deeplytics filled out with the requested uplift and proposed billing cadence.

We need to align on term length (we're leaning 24 months) and a couple of legal items before procurement can issue PO:
- Data retention / deletion language for cached KV data
- SLA credits application for burst capacity
- A short confidentiality addendum for model weights exchange during onboarding

Can you loop in legal and let us know an estimated timeline to get a redlined MSA back? Our procurement lead is procurement@deeplytics.com.

Attachments: Deeplytics_OrderForm_2027-05-03.pdf (pdf)

Best,
Sam Torres
Head of ML Ops, Deeplytics


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <sam.torres@deeplytics.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-04T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal + 60% capacity increase — next steps for paperwork

Sam — thanks for the clear summary and the order form. We're excited to support the uplift. Quick outline of next steps from our side:

1) Redwood will generate an updated Order Form + redline to our standard MSA and return to you by EOD 2027-05-06.
2) Ben (legal) — please review the two customer asks (data retention / SLA credits) and flag any outbound negotiation positions.
3) Monica (CS/ops) — I'll work with you to confirm the exact instance sizing behind the 60% uplift (seats, GPU types, KV storage) so the order form matches provisioning.
4) Laura (revops) — can you prepare billing cadence options (monthly vs quarterly vs annual prepay) and call out any promo/credit we can offer for 24-month term?

From Redwood's perspective, a 24-month term is fine — we usually ask to keep indemnity and liability caps unchanged. For data retention, we can offer a 90-day KV cache wipe by default, with an option to contractually shorten to 30 days if necessary (would like legal to confirm language). For SLA credits on burst traffic, we prefer preserving the existing credit metric (monthly window) but can add an explicit carve for burst overage triggers.

Proposed call: 2027-05-06 10:00 PT (30m) to run through the redlines and procurement path. Does that work for your team? If procurement prefers email-only redlines, we'll accept a tracked-change doc back.

Attachments: Redwood_MSA_v3_draft_redline.docx (redline)

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-05T08:40:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: Deeplytics paperwork — quick legal points

Aisha / Ben / Laura — quick internal note after skimming Sam's order form:

- Data retention: counter-propose the 90-day default but add an optional Exhibit X for 30-day retention if customer requires. That keeps the template predictable and allows a one-off carve.
- SLA credits: keep monthly uptime window, but add language that credits do not apply to burst provisioning events triggered by customer-originated spikes (so we can rely on autoscale billing).
- Confidentiality addendum: small tweak to Section 5.2 to allow short-term access to model artifacts for onboarding, conditioned on a narrower purpose clause and audit logs.

Ben — can you prepare 2-3 terse alternatives for each point we can provide to Deeplytics next call? Also include an estimate if any of these materially change liability exposure (we should flag to Aisha if so).

Quoted from Sam's email for context:
> We need to align on term length (we're leaning 24 months) and a couple of legal items before procurement can issue PO:
> - Data retention / deletion language for cached KV data
> - SLA credits application for burst capacity
> - A short confidentiality addendum for model weights exchange during onboarding

Monica
Senior Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Elena Varga <elena.varga@deeplytics.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Sam Torres <sam.torres@deeplytics.com>, procurement@deeplytics.com
Date: 2027-05-10T15:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal + 60% capacity increase — next steps for paperwork

Aisha — apologies for the delay, procurement got pulled into an audit last week. We reviewed Redwood's draft and have attached our redline to the MSA (Deeplytics_MSA_redline_v2.docx). High level:

- We accepted the 24-month term.
- Data retention: proposed stronger deletion guarantees (30 days) — we included language in the redline.
- SLA credits: we're willing to accept the monthly window but want explicit language that credits also apply if latency SLA misses occur during provisioning windows.
- Confidentiality addendum: attached a short addendum; procurement insists on a narrow scope and additional logging requirements.

Procurement asked that legal and CS route follow-ups via email and that we agree the final doc by 2027-05-14 to allow PO issuance and July 1 provisioning.

Attachments: Deeplytics_MSA_redline_v2.docx (docx, tracked changes)

title: Data control checkpoints + DDQ sprint plan
Tail latency budgeting & GPU mapping — RFI response
preview:
From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-01T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the quick intake call this morning. Attached is a short extract from our RFP that focuses on availability and tail-latency commitments. Our infra team is especially concerned about P95 vs P99 tail behavior for short prompt workloads (<=32 tokens output) and large context chat sessions (up to 8k tokens).

Specific asks:
- For reserved capacity, can you indicate per-GPU tail latency distributions (P50/P95/P99) for both A100-80GB and H100-80GB under our workload mix (see attachment)?
- How do you map failover to lower-cost GPUs (e.g., L4 / T4 class) while protecting tail latency and correctness?
- Do you have recommended reservation blocks for mixed workloads (bursty short prompts + sustained long-context chat) that minimize wasted reserved hours?

We'd appreciate a short written reply and a workbook we can use to model our reservations.

Best,
Sophie
Sophie Miller
Head of Infra Ops — Acme Health

Attachments: acme_tail_latency_requirements.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-01T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie —

Thanks for sending this over. I'm looping in Marcus (runtime perf) and Priyom (capacity planning) to provide the numbers and a workbook you can drop your mixes into. Expect the initial write-up by EOD Monday. A few clarifying notes:

- When you say "protecting tail latency and correctness" for fallback GPUs, do you mean strict P99 guarantees or an SLO that allows graceful degradation with a capped number of degraded requests that return a compatible model variant?
- For your short-prompt mix, can you confirm the average batch size and concurrency target (we can model 1:1 concurrency and optimistic batching separately)?

I'll send a meeting invite if that would be helpful, but we'll aim to reply in the thread first.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T14:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Sophie —

We ran quick microbenchmarks and modeled two representative profiles from your attachment:

Profile A: short prompts (<=32 output tokens), median input length ~30 tokens, concurrency target 200 qps
Profile B: long-context chat (8k context, 256 token outputs), concurrency target 30 qps

High-level results (single-request, cold KV cache):
- A100-80GB (fp16 / quant-aware kernels): P50 18ms, P95 42ms, P99 95ms for Profile A at 200 qps with continuous batching tuned to 80ms latency budget.
- H100-80GB (bf16): P50 13ms, P95 30ms, P99 68ms for Profile A — H100 shows consistently lower tail due to faster attention kernels and larger L2.
- For Profile B (long-context), both GPUs see higher medians: A100 P50 120ms / P95 260ms / P99 420ms; H100 P50 95ms / P95 210ms / P99 360ms.

Fallbacks and mapping guidance:
- We do not recommend falling back to L4/T4 for latency-sensitive P99 targets on short prompts unless you accept a higher P99 or perform model quantization + distilled variants. L-class GPUs are cost-effective for background batch/embedding workloads but have higher variance for low-latency web requests.
- Preferred strategy: reserve a primary pool on H100 for latency SLOs and maintain a small warm A100 pool as an intermediate tier. Use policy-based routing: route high-priority traffic to H100; non-critical low-cost requests to L4.
- Graceful degradation: automatic model-variant fallback (same-architecture distilled model) plus optional chunked response streaming to hide latency spikes.

Reservation heuristics we recommend (starter plan for your mixed workload):
- H100 pool: size to reach sustained 30 qps for long-context + headroom for 20% burst (estimate 8 H100 nodes).
- A100 pool: cover remaining short-prompt capacity for steady-state 180 qps (estimate 6 A100 nodes), configured with aggressive batching window and prefix caching.
- On-call burst policy: enable 12-hour burst capacity using shared GPU burst credits to handle unexpected spikes without overcommitting reserved hours.

I've attached a blank capacity workbook you can use to plug in your exact qps, concurrency, and percent-burst assumptions. The workbook includes sheets for kernel mix, per-model token cost, and tail-latency budget allocation.

Happy to walk through numbers live.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Senior Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx


From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-06T08:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Thanks Marcus — this is very helpful. A couple follow-ups after reviewing the workbook: 

1) For the H100 pool you recommend "8 nodes" for long-context. Can you surface the utilization threshold you used to size that? Is that 70% GPU util or 50%? We need to map to our SLA burn-rate for reserved hours.
2) If we enable auto-burst credits for 12 hours, how do you meter and bill that? Is burst billed at reserved rate or on-demand?
3) We're curious about tail-latency isolation: if one tenant in our multi-tenant namespace floods the prefix cache and causes KV miss storms, how does Redwood surface/limit that to meet P99 for others?

Would appreciate short answers inline and we can reconvene if needed.

Best,
Sophie


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-09T16:38:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie — quick inline replies and a couple of examples from the workbook.

1) Sizing utilization assumption: we sized H100 nodes to an average target of ~60% sustained GPU util for long-context sessions. That gives ~25% headroom for KV-cache churn and unexpected attention kernel variance while keeping reserved-hour efficiency reasonable. You can change the sheet to 50%/70% to see cost impact; the workbook has those toggles.

2) Billing for burst credits: burst is billed at the committed reserved rate up to your committed capacity; additional burst beyond your committed burst allowance is billed at on-demand pricing. The workbook's "Billing" tab models 12-hour daily burst allowance vs ad-hoc burst, and outputs monthly cost estimates. We also include an example contract clause for enterprise agreements to cap burst overage.

3) Tail-latency isolation & KV storms: engineering controls we deploy:
   - Per-tenant prefix cache quotas and eviction priorities; if a tenant triggers a high miss rate we'll throttle that tenant's new prefix caching for a short recovery window.
   - Request-level QoS: mark SLO-critical requests and route them to isolated worker pools with reserved KV cache regions.
   - Real-time alerts: when miss rate for a tenant exceeds threshold, we emit an auto page to infra-ops and move the tenant to a protected pool until manual review.

Attached is a filled example scenario using your sample traffic numbers. If you want, we can run your real traffic trace in our sandbox to produce a drop-in sizing recommendation.

Next steps:
- I'll share the completed workbook with sample toggles and a short 30-minute walkthrough (optional).
- Ava, can you coordinate contracting touchpoints if Acme wants to pursue a Dedicated commitment?

Thanks,
Priyom Das
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx (scenario example)

title: Temporary SLO accommodation request — dedicated pool migration (Solaris Energy)
preview:
From: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Meera Patel <meera.patel@solarisen.com>
Date: Mon, 03 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window

Hi Ava,

We’re kicking off a planned model reindex and dataset backfill next month that will shift a large portion of inference traffic into a short window (estimated peak ~8x our baseline for ~36 hours). The migration is customer-driven (internal analytics backfill + embeddings regen) and can’t be fully throttled without impacting regulatory reporting.

We want to avoid triggering Dedicated pool SLO breaches during the window. Can Redwood consider a temporary SLO accommodation or explicit carveout for the migration window (we’re targeting May 20–22 UTC)? If an accommodation isn’t possible, please outline the mitigations you can enact (pre-warm, burst capacity, prioritized queues) and what commercial remediation would look like if we get a breach.

Attached is a high-level migration timeline (preview) — I can send a full runbook once we lock dates.

Thanks,
Derek Shaw
Director of Platform Engineering
Solaris Energy

Attachment: Solaris_migration_preview.pdf

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 03 May 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window

Hi Derek — thanks for the heads-up and the preview. We can support Solaris during a short, planned migration, but we need a clear migration window, runbook, and an agreed mitigation + commercial plan. Quick notes: 

- Technical mitigations we can apply: pre-warm reserved nodes, enable temporary burst slices on your Dedicated pool, and create a migration-priority queue with explicit concurrency caps to limit cross-tenant impact.
- Operational constraints: we cannot compromise baseline throughput for unrelated customer traffic; any temporary carveout will be time-limited and instrumented.
- Commercial: typical approach is an SLA addendum + one-time invoice credit tied to the migration window.

I’ve looped in Markus and Karthik to capture technical and commercial constraints. I’m attaching our SLO_Waiver_Template_v1.pdf so we can iterate quickly — please send the detailed runbook and confirm the exact UTC window you’d like us to cover. Proposed next step: you send the runbook by EOD May 7, we’ll return a config + credit proposal by May 10.

Best,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Accounts — Dedicated Capacity
Redwood Inference

Attachment: SLO_Waiver_Template_v1.pdf

From: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 08:32:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window — technical proposal

Ava / team — wrote up a concise technical mitigation we can propose to Solaris. Key points: 

1) Dedicated pool behaviour: we use per-customer request queues with token-bucket shaping. By default we reserve a baseline capacity of GPUs and throughput that maps to the committed concurrency.

2) Migration carveout approach:
   - Reserve baseline capacity = 60% of current committed throughput for production-critical traffic.
   - Allow controlled burst window: configure a temporary \"migration slice\" that permits up to 3x nominal tokens/sec for 48 hours, split into rolling 6-hour ramp windows to avoid immediate tail pressure.
   - Enforce per-request priority: migration jobs assigned lower priority but with guaranteed minimal concurrency so they don’t cause head-of-line blocking.

3) Queueing/behavioral safeguards:
   - Tail-drop protection on best-effort queue to prevent eviction of high-priority inference traffic.
   - KV-cache pre-warm on a subset of nodes to reduce token compute overhead during peak.
   - Real-time metrics stream + alerting (latency p50/p95/p99, token/sec, queue depth) — we’ll create a shared Grafana board and Slack channel.

4) Risk and rollback: if we see cross-tenant latency regressions >15% sustained for 5 minutes, the migration slice is automatically scaled down and we fall back to baseline routing.

I’ve attached a minimal patch for queueing config to apply (queueing_config_patch.diff). If Solaris agrees to the ramp schedule we can pre-stage the config on May 18 and flip it on the morning of May 20 UTC.

— Markus
Senior Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: queueing_config_patch.diff

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 06 May 2027 16:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window — commercial terms

Derek — following Ava/Markus’ notes, here’s a commercial proposal for the migration addendum: 

- Duration: 48 hours (May 20 00:00 UTC — May 22 00:00 UTC) with optional 12-hour rollback buffer.
- Technical guardrails: as Markus outlined (60% baseline reservation + 3x controlled burst with ramping).
- Compensation: one-time credit of 12% of the monthly Dedicated invoice proportional to the migration window (applied to the next billing cycle).
- SLA addendum & signature: we’ll send an SLA addendum that captures the temporary carveout, rollback triggers, monitoring obligations, and the credit calculation.

I’m including a DocuSign link for the addendum — if you agree, sign and we’ll schedule the staging run on May 18. If Solaris needs a different credit level we can discuss with Finance, but this is our standard mitigated-waiver offer for large but short migrations.

DocuSign: https://docusign.net/Signing/Start/ABCDEF123456

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

From: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Meera Patel <meera.patel@solarisen.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 09 May 2027 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window — acceptance

Ava / Karthik — confirmed, Solaris accepts the proposed technical guardrails and the 12% one-time credit. We’ve signed the SLA addendum via DocuSign and uploaded the detailed migration runbook (attached).

Next asks from our side: please provision the pre-stage config on May 18 and confirm a daily check-in cadence (30 min stand) for May 20–22. Also confirm the shared dashboard URL and Slack channel name so our on-call can be invited.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — looking forward to a smooth migration.

Regards,
Derek Shaw
Director of Platform Engineering
Solaris Energy

Attachments: Solaris_migration_runbook_v3.xlsx

title: Renewal: AuroraAI capacity reprice & alignment
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@auroraai.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Renewal: AuroraAI capacity reprice & alignment

Laura —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. AuroraAI’s renewal is up in August and you mentioned a mid-contract capacity increase for Q3 models (roughly 2x current reserved tokens). Before I loop Legal/Finance, I wanted to propose a commercial structure that addresses your objectives: predictable unit cost for burst traffic, alignment of renewal dates, and minimal disruption to production. High level: 

- Option A (Conservative): Add 2x reserved capacity as a separate incremental SKU at 12% discount to list for the incremental commit; keep current renewal date (Aug 31) with proration for the remainder of the year.
- Option B (Co-term + Annual): Add 2x reserved capacity and co-term to a new annual renewal date (Jul 31) — overall blended discount 18% if Aurora commits to a 12-month co-term for the combined footprint. This reduces unit price and simplifies billing.

I’ve attached a short workbook with rough monthly proration/throughput assumptions. If you’re open, I can run a precise proration and create a DocuSign-ready amendment. What matters most: do you prefer keeping the current renewal cadence (Aug) or co-term to simplify ops and get a deeper blended discount?

Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0182
Redwood Inference

Attachments: AuroraAI_Renewal_Proposal_20270505.pdf, pricing-workbook-AUR-20270505.xlsx


From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@auroraai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: John Proc <john.proc@auroraai.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal: AuroraAI capacity reprice & alignment

Ben —

Thanks for the clear options. High level response: we want the better unit economics (so Option B is attractive) but we can’t lock into a larger annual commit without procurement sign-off on how you handle mid-year usage swings. A couple clarifying Qs: 

1) If we co-term to Jul 31 and usage falls below the blended commit, what flexibility do we have for a tiered true-up vs. overage?
2) Can you show the net price on our historical Q4 2026 traffic so we can see the blended impact?
3) We’ll need procurement timelines — they’ll want DocuSign + PO.

If you can provide a short contract amendment with both options and the proration math, I’ll push this to procurement.

—Laura
VP Product, AuroraAI
laura.chen@auroraai.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 14:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: AuroraAI renewal — quick pricing ask

Evelyn / Monica —

Forwarding Aurora’s reply. They like Option B (co-term + deeper blended price) but want clarity on true-up mechanics and conservative protections if usage drops. Can we do the following:

- Incremental capacity priced at a 15% discount on the incremental portion, but provide blended 18% if they commit to co-term annual for combined footprint (this matches internal ILR targets).
- Add a quarterly true-up with a 3-month lookback and tiered true-up bands (0-80% usage band = option to reduce at next quarter with 30-day notice; 80-110% band = true-up billing; >110% = overage at pay-as-you-go).
- Legal: short amendment language to allow mid-contract reduction once per 12-month period with 30-day notice (to appease procurement).

I attached the pricing workbook I shared with Laura. Want to confirm finance/legal comfortable with that structure before I send a firm amendment.

Thanks — Ben

Attachment: pricing-workbook-AUR-20270505.xlsx


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 10:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: AuroraAI renewal — quick pricing ask

Ben —

This structure is reasonable and aligns with our mid-market cadence. A few edits I recommend before customer-facing amendment: 

- Incremental discount: set at 15% (on incremental units) with the blended 18% only if they co-term and prepay at least 3 months of the incremental commit. That gives us a cash-protection lever.
- True-up bands: keep your proposed bands, but make reduction window 60 days notice (not 30). Procurement commonly asks for shorter windows; 60 days is defensible operationally.
- Overage: set pay-as-you-go at list less 5% (so there’s a small incentive to stay within committed bands).
- Legal to prepare a 1-page amendment and a one-sentence fallback: if Aurora requests capacity reduction within the first 90 days post-add, allow a one-time pro-rata credit to avoid churn risk.

If Finance signs off, I’m okay to approve for Ben to send to Aurora.

—Evelyn
Director, Sales Ops (Pricing)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@auroraai.com>
Cc: John Proc <john.proc@auroraai.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 12:00:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal: AuroraAI capacity reprice & alignment

Laura / John —

Thanks — appreciate the prompt feedback. Per internal alignment (Evelyn / Finance / Legal), here are two concrete offer paths. I attached the priced amendment and a workbook with the proration math.

Option 1 — Conservative (no co-term):
- Add 2x incremental reserved capacity as a separate SKU
- Incremental units priced at 15% discount to list
- Renewal stays Aug 31, 2027; incremental SKU is prorated to align with that renewal date
- Quarterly true-up bands: 0-80% (opt to reduce, 60 days notice); 80-110% (true-up billing); >110% (overage at list-5%)

Option 2 — Co-term and better blended price (preferred for unit economics):
- Combine incremental and existing commit, co-term to Jul 31, 2028
- Blended discount 18% applied to combined footprint if Aurora pre-pays at least 3 months of the incremental commit at signing (prepay holds price for 30 days)
- Same true-up bands and one-time 90-day pro-rata credit protection for reductions within first 90 days

Next steps: I can prepare a DocuSign amendment for Option 2 with the prepay checkbox, or send Option 1 amendment if you prefer no co-term. If you want finance to draft a PO, I’ll include exact invoice amounts and vendor order codes. Pricing-hold: we can hold these prices through EOD 05/11 if we have a verbal confirmation.

Would you like me to route the DocuSign to John for procurement and you for signature?

Ben

Attachments: AuroraAI_Renewal_Proposal_20270505.pdf, pricing-workbook-AUR-20270505.xlsx


From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@auroraai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: John Proc <john.proc@auroraai.com>
Date: Fri, 7 May 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal: AuroraAI capacity reprice & alignment

Ben —

Thanks for the clear options and the workbook — Option 2 looks like the right path for us. We’ll take the co-term with the blended 18% and proceed with the 3-month prepay as discussed. Please route the DocuSign to me and John; procurement will produce a PO once the signature is complete.

Can you include a one-line clause describing the quarterly true-up bands and the 90-day one-time pro-rata protection so procurement can see it up front? Otherwise procurement tends to back-and-forth on the details.

We’ll aim to sign next week. Appreciate the quick turn.

Best —
Laura

title: Lumenia post-incident readout, corrective actions, and closure register
preview:
From: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Cc: lumenia-ops@lumenia.com
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Ben / Neha,

We just saw a significant spike in 5xx rates and end-user latency across two of our retail customers starting ~08:50 PDT. Our observability shows tokenization errors and request timeouts against the Redwood API — pages are timing out and customers are opening tickets. We're on a Zoom with our SREs but wanted to loop you in to request an urgent SLA credit discussion and action plan.

Key customer impact:
- Checkout timeouts for ~4% of traffic for Horizon Retail (high-revenue).
- Latency tail increased from 150ms to 2.3s for text-generation calls.

Can Redwood confirm root cause and ETA for mitigation? We need an exec readout for our CTO at 11:00 PDT.

Thanks,
Michael Dunne
Head of Platform Operations, Lumenia

[attachments: incident-snippet.log]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:25:33 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Michael — thank you for the heads up and sorry for the disruption. We're on it. Initial triage indicates a combination of a degraded routing decision in one regional pool and a KV-cache eviction storm after a model rollout this morning.

Immediate actions we have taken:
- Rolled back the morning model variant at 09:18 PDT (Karthik).
- Forced rehydration of KV caches and increased cache timeouts to avoid repeated misses (Neha).
- Isolated serving pool A in us-west-2 and re-routed new traffic to pool B while we validate (Marissa).

I will join your 11:00 CTO readout and prepare an initial impact brief + timeline. Sharing a lightweight RCA and charts in the shared drive in 30–45 minutes.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
[attachments: none referenced]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: lumenia-ops@lumenia.com, Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 10:05:10 -07:00
Subject: RE: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Team — quick technical summary for the record (will add to RCA doc):
- Trigger: morning canary introduced a micro-optimization in token streaming that altered routing keys for a sharded KV layer. Some hot prefixes experienced cache churn.
- Symptom: repeated remote cache misses resulted in synchronous recomputation and elevated p95/p99; combined with a routing cold-start to a smaller pool led to 5xx exhaustion on pool A.
- Fix applied: reverted canary, increased cache TTLs, drained pool A, and bumped pool B capacity by +15% for traffic smoothing.

Next steps I own: validate p99 back to baseline across Horizon Retail traffic, add guardrail preventing routing-key changes during canary windows, and produce post-incident code-level changelog. ETA: EOD 05/04 for p99 validation; full changelog 05/06.

-Karthik
Sr. Manager, Serving Runtime

> On Tue, May 4, 2027 at 9:25 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Michael — thank you for the heads up and sorry for the disruption...

From: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 11:12:40 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Lumenia post-incident readout materials (RCA + graphs)

Michael — attaching the RCA PDF and the latency graphs. High-level impact summary is at the top. We included a temporary SLA credit proposal (2% of this month's invoice applied as service credit) and a proposed remediation timeline.

Forwarded message:

From: Redwood Incident Ops <incident-ops@redwood.com>
To: ben.carter@redwood.com
Date: Tue, May 4, 2027 11:03:15 -07:00
Subject: Lumenia — RCA draft and graphs attached

Attached: rfc-20270504-lumenia-rca.pdf, latency-graphs-05-04.png

Summary excerpt:
- Root cause: caching churn triggered by routing-key change during canary + smaller-than-expected pool capacity during peak surge.
- Impact window: 08:48 — 09:30 PDT; affected tokens ~3.8% of requests for Horizon Retail.
- Immediate mitigations: rollback canary, increased cache TTL, forced pool drain; monitoring confirmed p99 back to baseline by 09:45 PDT.
- Proposed long-term fixes: routing-key immutability during canaries, automated pool headroom policy, and KV-layer instrumentation.

Neha Kapoor
Customer Reliability Lead, Redwood Inference
[attachments: rfc-20270504-lumenia-rca.pdf; latency-graphs-05-04.png]

From: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Cc: lumenia-ops@lumenia.com, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 12:00:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Lumenia post-incident readout materials (RCA + graphs)

Thanks — received and sharing internally. Couple of asks before the exec readout at 15:00 PDT: can you quantify financial exposure (approx. revenue impacted) and confirm SLA credit proposal is on the table? Also, can Grace join the 15:00 readout for commercial-level commitments?

Michael

> On Tue, May 4, 2027 at 11:12 AM Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Michael — attaching the RCA PDF and the latency graphs...

From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 13:05:22 -07:00
Subject: RE: Lumenia post-incident readout, corrective actions, and closure register

Michael — I can join the 15:00 readout. On credits: we will offer a provisional service credit of 2% for May invoice and will finalize after the full post-incident review. Additionally, we'll commit to the following remediation milestones (we'll put these in the shared checklist and report weekly until closure):
- 05/06: routing immutability guard + unit tests (owner: Karthik).
- 05/12: automated pool headroom policy deployed to canary and early-warning alarms (owner: Marissa).
- 05/20: KV-layer instrumentation with heatmap and per-prefix metrics (owner: Neha).

I will own commercial communications and finalize credit language with Lumenia after the readout.

Grace O'Connor
Head of Customer Success, Redwood Inference

[signature block omitted for brevity]

From: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
Cc: lumenia-ops@lumenia.com
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Ben / Neha,

We just saw a significant spike in 5xx rates and end-user latency across two of our retail customers starting ~08:50 PDT. Our observability shows tokenization errors and request timeouts against the Redwood API — pages are timing out and customers are opening tickets. We're on a Zoom with our SREs but wanted to loop you in to request an urgent SLA credit discussion and action plan.

Key customer impact:
- Checkout timeouts for ~4% of traffic for Horizon Retail (high-revenue).
- Latency tail increased from 150ms to 2.3s for text-generation calls.

Can Redwood confirm root cause and ETA for mitigation? We need an exec readout for our CTO at 11:00 PDT.

Thanks,
Michael Dunne
Head of Platform Operations, Lumenia

[attachments: incident-snippet.log]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:25:33 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Michael — thank you for the heads up and sorry for the disruption. We're on it. Initial triage indicates a combination of a degraded routing decision in one regional pool and a KV-cache eviction storm after a model rollout this morning.

Immediate actions we have taken:
- Rolled back the morning model variant at 09:18 PDT (Karthik).
- Forced rehydration of KV caches and increased cache timeouts to avoid repeated misses (Neha).
- Isolated serving pool A in us-west-2 and re-routed new traffic to pool B while we validate (Marissa).

I will join your 11:00 CTO readout and prepare an initial impact brief + timeline. Sharing a lightweight RCA and charts in the shared drive in 30–45 minutes.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
[attachments: none referenced]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: lumenia-ops@lumenia.com, Michael Dunne <michael.dunne@lumenia.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 10:05:10 -07:00
Subject: RE: Urgent: Customer-side impact — sustained errors + latency spike

Team — quick technical summary for the record (will add to RCA doc):
- Trigger: morning canary introduced a micro-optimization in token streaming that altered routing keys for a sharded KV layer. Some hot prefixes experienced cache churn.
- Symptom: repeated remote cache misses resulted in synchronous recomputation and elevated p95/p99; combined with a routing cold-start to a smaller pool led to 5xx exhaustion on pool A.
- Fix applied: reverted canary, increased cache TTLs, drained pool A, and bumped pool B capacity by +15% for traffic smoothing.

Next steps I own: validate p99 back to baseline across Horizon Retail traffic, add guardrail preventing routing-key changes during canary windows, and produce post-incident code-level changelog. ETA: EOD 05/04 for p99 validation; full changelog 05/06.

-Karthik
Sr. Manager, Serving Runtime

title: Invoice exports, redaction proposal, and DSR interplay
preview:
From: Jill Harmon <jill.harmon@acme-corp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@acme-corp.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Data deletion request + invoices for Q1 (Acme)
Attachments: Acme_PO_Q1_2027.pdf (application/pdf)

Hi Ben,

Hope you’re well. We have an internal data subject request where one of our end users asked Acme to request deletion of their personal data from vendors. Can you confirm what Redwood’s process is for: 1) deleting user-identifying telemetry/usage tied to a specific account or API key, and 2) whether that deletion also removes invoices/usage records that our finance team requires for audit and procurement reconciliation?

From our side we need to make sure procurement can still match charges to PO/invoice numbers for Q1. If full deletion isn’t possible for invoices, can you explain what fields are retained and whether you can provide a redacted export that removes PII but preserves billing amounts and invoice numbers?

Appreciate a clear yes/no and recommended next steps — our auditors are on a tight timeline.

Thanks,
Jill Harmon
Procurement Manager
Acme Corp


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jill Harmon <jill.harmon@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@acme-corp.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Data deletion request + invoices for Q1 (Acme)
Attachments: redaction_guidance_template.pdf (application/pdf)

Hi Jill — thanks for the note and the PO. Short answer up front: we can remove or redact user-identifying telemetry tied to an account, but we cannot drop accounting ledger entries that are necessary for statutory record-keeping and audit reconciliation.

Details:
- Accounting/billing records (invoice numbers, dates, line-item charges, total amounts, and VAT/tax calculations) are retained separately in our finance ledger to satisfy US GAAP / tax and audit obligations. Typical retention here is 7 years for US-based contracts; your procurement/audit teams can rely on those records.
- Personal data fields (end-user emails, IP addresses, session tokens) that are stored in usage telemetry can be removed or pseudonymized on request. That means we can provide a redacted invoice export that preserves invoice IDs, amounts, and timestamps but removes or hashes direct identifiers.
- For customers that require a reconciliation mapping (to tie redacted IDs back to internal user IDs), we can provide a tokenization approach: we deliver a mapping file encrypted to your key under a limited retention window for your internal reconciliation, then securely delete the mapping. This keeps PII out of our primary invoice exports while enabling your finance team to match charges.

Next steps I recommend: 1) Finance/legal confirmation on the retention window (I’m looping in Neha and Marissa below), 2) We propose to deliver a redacted invoice export + optional encrypted reconciliation mapping, and 3) Execute via a signed SOP/acknowledgement so your auditors accept the format. I attached our redaction guidance template which shows the exact fields we remove vs keep.

If that approach works, say so and I’ll coordinate with finance to produce the export within 48 hours.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales – Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 09:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Data deletion request + invoices for Q1 (Acme)

Neha / Marissa — forwarding Acme’s procurement question and my proposed approach below. Quick asks from finance/legal:
1) Confirm the minimum statutory retention window we need to keep observable invoice/ledger records for a US-based customer (I referenced 7 years above — is that conservative enough for Redwood’s policy?),
2) Confirm the fields finance needs to preserve verbatim on the export so Acme’s audit team can reconcile (invoice_id, invoice_date, line_items with amounts, tax_amount, total),
3) Any objections to delivering a redacted export + encrypted reconciliation mapping (mapping only retained for 30 days) as our standard for DSRs that would otherwise touch billing records.

On the product side, the redaction guide attached matches how we’d strip PII from usage rows while keeping billing columns intact. If finance is comfortable, I’ll tell Acme we can produce the export and mapping.

Thanks — Ben

On Wed, 5 May 2027 at 11:05 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Jill — thanks for the note and the PO. Short answer up front: we can remove or redact user-identifying telemetry tied to an account, but we cannot drop accounting ledger entries that are necessary for statutory record-keeping and audit reconciliation.
>
> [snip - redaction guidance attached]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jill Harmon <jill.harmon@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@acme-corp.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 May 2027 16:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Data deletion request + invoices for Q1 (Acme)
Attachments: Acme_invoice_Q1_2027_redacted.csv (text/csv)

Hi Jill — looping back with the approved plan after confirming with finance/legal.

What we will deliver:
- A redacted invoice export (attached) that contains invoice_id, invoice_date, line_items (descriptions), amounts, tax, and total. All direct identifiers (end-user email, session IDs, IPs) are removed from the export.
- An encrypted reconciliation mapping file (we will encrypt with the public key you provide) that maps our internal usage IDs to a tokenized value on the redacted export. Redwood will retain that mapping for 30 days and then securely delete it; if you need a longer window, let us know and we’ll route for legal approval.
- A short audit bundle (PDF) explaining the redaction steps and a checksum of the export for your auditors.

Why this helps: preserves your ability to reconcile charges to PO/invoice numbers while honoring the end-user deletion request from a PII standpoint. Per our finance guidance, the ledger/invoice entries themselves are retained for statutory reasons (7 years).

If that plan works, please reply with a confirmation and provide a public key for the encrypted mapping. Once we have that, I’ll schedule the export and share the audit bundle within 48 hours.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales – Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

title: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure discussion
preview:
From: Amelia Ruiz <amelia@cloudmart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@cloudmart.com
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure

Ben —

Thanks for the demo earlier. Procurement asked me to put our high-level ask on email so we can move to legal/finance review. CloudMart is evaluating a private offer on Azure marketplace for our reseller channel. Two items we need from Redwood to make this workable: 

- A retainer-style monthly credit (we convert to consumption credits against Marketplace SKUs) — our target is $50k/mo for 12 months.
- A channel margin (pass-through) for our reseller partners. We typically push for 12% net to the channel.

Before we send this up to our finance team, can you confirm: 
1) Whether Redwood will accept a retainer-to-credit conversion on Azure private offer (i.e., Redwood invoices retainer, CloudMart converts as list-price credits)? 
2) If you can support a 12% channel margin on top of the private-offer list price, or if you have a standard channel fee cap.
3) Any minimum commit or SKU bundling requirements we should be aware of.

We'd like to get an indicative structure (numbers + sample billing flow) this week so our procurement can prepare the PO template.

Thanks,
Amelia Ruiz
Director, Strategic Partnerships
CloudMart Inc.

[Attachment: CloudMart_PO_Spec_v2.pdf (1 page)]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Amelia Ruiz <amelia@cloudmart.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 16:05:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure

Hi Amelia —

Really appreciate the clarity. Quick answers up front, followed by a couple clarifying Qs so we can produce a formal PO template: 

- Retainer -> credit: Yes, we can structure a monthly retainer that converts into Marketplace credits. We typically model this as an up-front invoice for the retainer that Redwood redeems as pre-paid consumption against the private-offer SKUs.
- Channel margin: We can support pass-through margins, but the % interacts with list-price anchoring and revenue recognition. Typical bands we approve are 8–12% depending on commit and ARR visibility.
- Minimums / bundling: We prefer at least three SKUs to avoid pass-through complexity (core, premium, support add-on) and a 3-month ramp to validate usage patterns.

Can you confirm whether the $50k/mo is fixed or if you expect a ramp (e.g., $25k -> $50k)? Also, do you need Redwood to accept customer-facing resale pricing (i.e., CloudMart sets end-customer price) or will Redwood's list price be published and CloudMart take a margin off that?

I'm attaching an initial pricing workbook with scenarios (retainer conversion math, margin impact, and a recommended anchor list price). Irene and Tessa are looped to review against our channel policy.

Best,
Ben Carter
Partner Sales, Marketplace
Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Redwood_PRICING_WORKBOOK.xlsx (pricing scenarios)]

From: Amelia Ruiz <amelia@cloudmart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@cloudmart.com
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 09:20:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure

Ben —

Thanks for the quick reply and the workbook — reviewed with procurement. Answers below: 

- The $50k/mo would be fixed for the first 6 months as our pilot commitment, then reviewed for scale. We can discuss a ramp for months 7–12 based on usage.
- CloudMart will operate as the reseller: we will set the end-customer price and take the margin. Redwood's list price will be published in the private-offer metadata and CloudMart will invoice customers separately.

Procurement's ask: can Redwood agree to a 12% channel margin pass-through for the pilot term (first 6 months) and then revert to a review clause after month 6? They also want the retainer to be refundable if monthly consumption is less than 40% of the retainer for two consecutive months (simple clawback).

If you can confirm these points we'll route to our Legal for a draft PO and T&Cs.

Cheers,
Amelia

On Wed, May 5, 2027 at 16:05 Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Amelia —
> 
> Really appreciate the clarity. Quick answers up front...

From: Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 11:33:00 +0000
Subject: Fwd: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure (internal analysis)

Ben / Irene —

Internal read on CloudMart's counter: good logo opportunity but margin pressure if we accept 12% and the $50k retainer with the clawback request. Attached is a quick scenario worksheet (same workbook updated) — two recommended paths: 

Option A (Conservative):
- Approve 10% channel pass-through for pilot (months 1–6).
- Accept $50k/mo retainer but change clawback to apply after three consecutive months under 40% usage.
- Redwood publishes list price; allow CloudMart to set downstream pricing.

Option B (Aggressive, volume-conditioned):
- Approve 12% channel pass-through only if CloudMart commits to a 9-month minimum and demonstrates 60% consumption by month 3.
- Provide a step-down credit (month 7–9) if they under-index.

I marked line items in the workbook with expected ARR impact and suggested accounting code for revenue recognition. Recommend we propose Option A to start, with an explicit review at month 4.

Tessa
Director, Marketplace Operations
Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Redwood_PRICING_WORKBOOK.xlsx (updated, internal)]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Amelia Ruiz <amelia@cloudmart.com>, procurement@cloudmart.com
Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 May 2027 14:08:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Private offer: retainer + channel fee structure

Amelia / procurement team —

Thanks for the details and for surfacing the clawback. After internal review we can do the following for the pilot term (formal PO language to follow): 

- Pilot term (months 1–6): Redwood will accept a $50,000/month retainer convertible to Azure private-offer credits.
- Channel margin: Redwood will permit a 10% pass-through margin for months 1–6.
- Clawback: Retainer refundable if consumption <40% for three consecutive months. We moved to a 3-month trigger to reduce churn risk.
- Review: At the end of month 4 we will run a joint usage review; if CloudMart is >=60% consumption by month 3 we will consider elevating to 12% for the remainder of the term (subject to an addendum).

I attached an updated PO spec reflecting these terms and a DocuSign link for an initial MOU so legal teams can start redlines. If procurement is comfortable with the 10% pilot margin and 3-month clawback trigger, please confirm and we'll route the MOU.

Docs: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/673219/DM-78321 | DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/en/link/CLDMRT-PO-DOC-892

Appreciate the collaboration — looking forward to getting the pilot moving.

Best,
Ben Carter
Partner Sales, Marketplace
Redwood Inference
[Attachment: CloudMart_PO_Spec_v2.pdf (updated)]

title: DPA & SCCs: delegation + transfer-impact questions for DataForge Labs order form
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@dataforge.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 3 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: DPA & SCCs: delegation + transfer-impact questions for DataForge Labs order form

Hi Elena —

Thanks again for the productive call earlier. Per our discussion, DataForge will be onboarding our managed inference offering and the legal team asked for a couple of clarifications so we can finalize the Order Form + MSA tie-outs:

1) You flagged a liability / data flow carveout in section 4 of the draft DPA. Can you confirm which subprocessors and regions that applies to (we're particularly trying to map EU transfers to the Redwood Private vs Hosted variants)?

2) You requested the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) be attached as an exhibit with a delegation matrix. If you can share the redline, we'll run it by privacy ops for delegation/processor vs controller mapping.

3) Procurement asked for an estimated evidence list for the SCCs (SOC 2 report timeframe, encryption-at-rest/transport statements, and KMS integration note). Can you share anything you already have?

If it's easier I can loop in Monica (privacy ops) and Rishi (contracts) to vet the redline. We've also prepared a short dataflow map based on the integration docs — attaching it for context.

Next steps: If you can send the redline and any subprocessor roster by EOD Thursday (5/6), we'll prioritize legal review and return an illustrated delegation matrix & open items.

Appreciate it —

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123
[Attachment: DataFlow_Map_v1.xlsx]

From: Elena Morales <elena.morales@dataforge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@dataforge.com>
Date: Tue, 3 May 2027 11:03:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: DPA & SCCs: delegation + transfer-impact questions for DataForge Labs order form

Hi Ben —

Thanks — attaching the redline of the DPA and a draft subprocessor roster. Highlights:
- We limited the carveout to training data pipelines only (see para 4.2). Production inference logs are in scope.
- We requested addition of a narrow transfer-impact addendum to clarify which party is responsible for SCC execution when we route EU traffic to third-party compute.
- We included an optional execution path that references customer-managed keys (KMIP/KMS) for on-prem usage.

Attachments: DataForge_DPA_redline_20270503.docx; Subprocessors_Roster_20270503.csv

Elena Morales
Head of Legal Operations | DataForge Labs
elena.morales@dataforge.com
[Attachment: DataForge_DPA_redline_20270503.docx]

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 3 May 2027 12:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: DPA redline from DataForge — quick intake and gaps

Forwarding DataForge's DPA redline + subprocessor roster. Key asks from Procurement/DF: carveout is limited to training pipelines, SCCs exhibit + transfer-impact addendum, optional CMK path.

Can privacy ops + contracts confirm:
- Whether the carveout language for \"training pipelines\" as redlined is acceptable from a product/ops standpoint (i.e., does training access ever touch customer-configured inference workloads)?
- Whether we can accept their proposed language that makes SCC execution the customer's responsibility when EU traffic is routed to a third-party controller. That feels like a unilateral delegation and may create post-execution audit liability.

I've uploaded the docs to our shared folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cDFDataForgeDPA

Ben

> 
> Thanks — attaching the redline of the DPA and a draft subprocessor roster. Highlights:
> - We limited the carveout to training data pipelines only (see para 4.2). Production inference logs are in scope.
> - We requested addition of a narrow transfer-impact addendum to clarify which party is responsible for SCC execution when we route EU traffic to third-party compute.
> - We included an optional execution path that references customer-managed keys (KMIP/KMS) for on-prem usage.
> 
> Attachments: DataForge_DPA_redline_20270503.docx; Subprocessors_Roster_20270503.csv
> 
> Elena Morales
> Head of Legal Operations | DataForge Labs
> [Attachment: DataForge_DPA_redline_20270503.docx]

From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 4 May 2027 10:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: DPA redline from DataForge — quick intake and gaps

Thanks for the loop. I reviewed the redline and flagged the following items in the attached extract (SCCs_clause_extract.pdf):

1) Training carveout: the draft as-written is ambiguous re: \"model maintenance\" vs \"training pipelines\". We should tighten to: \"training that includes customer-supplied personal data for model retraining; model updates that do not ingest customer personal data are in scope.\"

2) SCCs execution: DataForge's proposed transfer-impact addendum attempts to shift SCC onboarding and execution to the controller when tertiary compute providers are used. We should not accept a broad shift of SCC operational obligations. Proposed compromise language: allow customer to initiate SCC review but Redwood retains responsibility to ensure downstream subprocessors (including third-party compute partners we route traffic to) have executed SCCs or equivalent safeguards before routing.

3) Evidence list: SOC 2 (most recent report), KMS configuration note (if opting for CMK), subprocessor assurance statements. Monica: please confirm the minimum evidence we can accept and whether the KMS path requires additional security review.

I'll prepare a tracked redline of the DPA and an annotated delegation matrix (deliverable: DPA_comments_DataForge_20270504.docx) once we confirm the KMS/evidence ask.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts | Redwood Inference
laura@redwood.com
[Attachment: SCCs_clause_extract.pdf]

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@dataforge.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 6 May 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Next steps on DPA redline & evidence for DataForge Labs (DF-2027-0931)

Hi Elena —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the roster. Per legal + privacy ops review we propose the following approach and timeline:

1) Redwood will produce a tracked redline of the DPA (we will not accept a broad transfer-of-SCC-responsibility to the customer). Expect the redline by COB Friday (5/7).

2) Evidence list (minimal set): most recent SOC 2 Type II report (within last 12 months), confirmation of in-transit and at-rest encryption, subprocessor due-diligence memos for any third-party compute providers, and KMS integration note if DataForge elects the CMK path. Monica will confirm whether a separate penetration testing attestation is required.

3) Execution path: We can support an exhibit that documents routing options (Hosted -> Redwood endpoints in EU, Dedicated -> customer VPC, Private -> on-prem). For any route that uses a third-party controller, Redwood will require proof of SCCs or equivalent and will only route traffic after verifying vendor assurances.

4) DocuSign: we'll prepare and send an initial MSA/Order Form package with the Redwood-proposed DPA redline once internal approvals are complete. I'll share the DocuSign link when ready.

If that approach works for DataForge, please confirm and we will proceed. Thanks —

Ben Carter
