title: Throughput assurance & term-flex proposal for Dedicated GPU pool
preview:
From: Samira Khan <samira.k@orchidhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@orchidhealth.com
Date: 2027-05-03T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Post-pilot: committed capacity recommendation?

Hi Ben —

Thanks again for running the pilot last month. The team is happy with latency but we need to lock down committed capacity and commercial terms before we expand to production. A few specifics we want covered in the proposal:

- Sustained throughput target: ~350–450 inference qps (70–90 concurrent users, 8–12 tokens per request on average) with 95th pct latency under 250ms.
- Burst capacity for peak sessions (up to 800 qps for 10–15 minutes).
- Pricing bands for 12-month vs 24-month commits; flexibility to scale up/down after quarter 1.
- Any credits or transition support for model quantization/ops tuning.

I've attached the pilot_load_test_summary.pdf with our telemetry (traces and sample logs). Procurement is cc'd and will need an estimated MRR for budgeting.

Would appreciate a written recommendation and a commercial worksheet we can review with finance.

Best,
Samira
Director of Product
Orchid Health

Attachment: pilot_load_test_summary.pdf (PDF, 1.2MB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira.k@orchidhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T13:48:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-pilot: committed capacity recommendation?

Hi Samira —

Thanks for the clear asks and for the pilot data. We pulled the telemetry into our sizing tool and I copied Marissa (SE) and Laura (sales lead) to help with the commercial side. Quick notes up front:

- The pilot traces look congruent with a 400 qps sustained target if we allocate ~8 A100-equivalent GPUs (our A100-equivalent metric factors in quantization benefits you saw).
- For brief spikes to ~800 qps we recommend burst credits (short-lived surplus capacity) coupled with autoscaling rules that cap cost exposure.
- We can offer a term-flex pricing structure (12 mo vs 24 mo) with tiered discounts and an explicit scale-up window in Q1.

Marissa will follow up with the sizing calc and a recommended MRR band; Laura will attach a commercial worksheet. If possible, can procurement share the internal budget window and whether a single PO or monthly invoicing is preferred?

I'll be on the call Friday to walk through the details — suggest 30 minutes.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555-0182



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-04T10:22:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: sizing notes & recommended term-flex structure (notes for Samira)

Team —

I dug into the attached pilot summary and ran it against our production-equivalent load simulator. Short version and recommended commercial guardrails:

Sizing recommendation (conservative):
- Baseline: 8 A100-equivalent GPUs to sustain 400 qps at p95 latency ~180–220ms (model: open-gpt-large, sequence avg 12 tokens).
- Burst headroom: additional 4 GPU-equivalents as "burst credits" that can be used for 15-minute windows; handled via transient capacity pool.
- KV-cache hit patterns in the pilot show a 12% effective token saving; we baked that into the calc.

Commercial proposal (term-flex):
- 12-month commit: 20% list discount off hosted-dedicated baseline; includes 500k token credits for ops tuning + 8 hours of SE support for quantization and runtime tuning.
- 24-month commit: 28% list discount, 3 months of burst credits included, and prioritized slot reservations during region maintenance windows.
- Add-on: option to convert burst credits to permanent capacity at pro‑rated rates within first 120 days.

SLA/guarantees:
- Sustained throughput guarantee: We commit to 400 qps (±5%) at p95 latency under normal operating load with an availability SLA of 99.9% for Dedicated pool.
- Exclusions: sustained DDoS, or customer-driven load tests > 2x committed average unless pre-notified.

Attached is sizing_calcs.xlsx with the per-GPU qps envelope and a commercial_termflex_v2.xlsx draft. Rafael — please sanity-check the discount math and margin impact; I'm showing ~32% gross margin at 20% list discount for a 12-mo.

I'll prepare a short one-pager for Samira summarizing the trade-offs (commit vs burst).

Thanks,
Marissa
Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: sizing_calcs.xlsx (Excel, 45KB); commercial_termflex_v2.xlsx (Excel, 22KB)

From: procurement@orchidhealth.com <procurement@orchidhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Samira Khan <samira.k@orchidhealth.com>
Date: 2027-05-05T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Throughput assurance & term-flex proposal for Dedicated GPU pool

Ben / Samira —

Thanks for the draft. A couple procurement items we need clarified before we can approve:

1) Price protection: if we commit 12 months at the 20% discount, will there be protection against upward list price changes for that term? We need a firm not-to-exceed price.
2) Early termination: standard termination with pro-rated refunds is acceptable but we need language for a 90-day notice and a cap on termination fees.
3) SLA credits: define methodology for SLA measurement and credit calculation.

Attached is our standard_vendor_terms_and_security.zip (excerpted questionnaire attached as orchid_security_questionnaire.docx).

Regarding discount: our finance team is asking for 25% as a target for 12 months if we can get price protection and a modest termination cap. If you can get within 22–24% with the SLA language, we can likely sign.

Please advise — we need to finalize the MRR by next Tuesday for the May board packet.

Regards,
Procurement
Orchid Health

Attachment: orchid_security_questionnaire.docx (DOCX, 150KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: procurement@orchidhealth.com <procurement@orchidhealth.com>, Samira Khan <samira.k@orchidhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-05-06T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Throughput assurance & term-flex proposal for Dedicated GPU pool

Procurement team / Samira —

Appreciate the clarity. We've updated the commercial worksheet and the legal/ops guardrails to reflect your asks. Key changes in v2:

- Price protection: committed MRR will be locked for the duration of the agreed term (12 or 24 months) — list increases do not affect the committed price.
- Early termination: 90-day notice is acceptable; termination fee capped at the greater of (3 months committed MRR) or actual usage delta for the quarter.
- SLA credits: measured at the Dedicated pool level using token/latency sampling; credits calculated as % of monthly MRR (standard table included in the worksheet).

Commercial concessions offered:
- 12-month commit: 22% discount off list, includes 8 hours SE tuning + 500k token credits.
- 24-month commit: 30% discount, 3 months burst credits included, and reserved slot priority.

Next steps: I attached commercial_termflex_v2.xlsx (final draft) and a DocuSign envelope link for the Statement of Work + Order Form. If Orchid signs the 12-month at 22% today, we can schedule the private onboarding runbook session next week and lock the burst credit pool.

DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/prepare/DS-20270503-ORCH
HubSpot: HS-14287
Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2B3C4D5E

Happy to jump on a 20-minute call this afternoon to finalize.

Best,
Ben Carter

title: Next steps: Security runbook & MAP checkpoints for ClearPoint evaluation
preview:
From: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@clearpointbank.com>, Eli Turner <eli.turner@auditpartner.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Our security team wants to formalize a short MAP so we can evaluate Redwood for a production pilot. High-level asks from our side: SOC 2 attestation (or timeline to ship evidence), SIG/CAIQ responses, an initial DPA we can redline, and confirmation of data retention/deletion SLAs for caching and logs.

Proposed cadence: kickoff the week of May 9, SIG bulk review the following week, DPA redline by end of week 3, SOC2 evidence review week 4. Can you share a short runbook/MAP with owners on your side and any standard documents you will attach?

Attaching our internal primer to give context.

Thanks,
Anna
-----
ClearPoint Security Primer (attachment): ClearPoint_Security_Primer.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Anna — thanks for the clear ask and the primer. Attached is a concise MAP + runbook and our baseline artifacts to accelerate your review. High level plan below (happy to iterate):

- Week 0 (May 9): Kickoff call — agenda: scope, sequence lengths, data residency, and acceptance criteria. Owner: Ava (AM).
- Week 1 (May 10–14): SIG/CAIQ Q&A bulk delivery — Owner: Marcus (runtime/security tech lead). We'll provide an initial completed CAIQ spreadsheet and highlighted items for you to focus on.
- Week 2 (May 17–21): DPA draft + procurement coordination — Owner: Naomi (legal ops). We will deliver DPA_draft_v1.docx for redline and propose standard indemnities/retention clauses.
- Week 3 (May 24–28): SOC 2 evidence & operational questions — Owner: Ben (compliance). We will surface a redacted SOC 2 summary and evidence index.
- Week 4 (May 31–Jun 4): Acceptance review and go/no-go decision.

Key security touchpoints to confirm now:
- Data retention for KV cache and ephemeral runtime state (we propose TTL=30 days by default, configurable per-customer).
- Log retention: we retain operational logs for 12 months; customer-managed log exports available on request.
- DPA: we support deletion requests and can add explicit SLA language for deletion and attestations.

Attachments: Redwood_SOC2_summary.pdf, DPA_draft_v1.docx, SIG-CAIQ-responses.xlsx

Links: https://docs.redwood.ai/shared/MAP-ClearPoint-2027 (MAP draft)

Next steps: confirm kickoff slot (I suggested Tue May 10 at 10am PT in HubSpot) and let us know if you need a private deployment discussion up front.

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | (415) 555-0182


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T08:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Adding a few tech notes for the SIG call and MAP so we don't get downstream surprises:

- KV cache/data retention: our default TTL is 30 days but we can offer 0-day (no cache) or shorter windows for sensitive workloads; explain tradeoffs to the ClearPoint security team on latency/cost.

title: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>
To: Legal <legal@edgehealth.com>, security@edgehealth.com
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Hi team — thanks again for taking the time on Friday. Per our call, I wanted to start a quick thread to capture two narrow items EdgeHealth asked about so we can get answers back to legal/security.

1) KMS/export-controls: Edge asked whether customer-managed keys can be used for PII and whether any subprocessors will ever have access to plaintext during model serving or logs.
2) Subprocessor reachability & dataflow: they’d like a concise inventory and a diagram that shows which subprocessors touch identifiable data, where it is encrypted, and whether keys or tokenized blobs cross third-party boundaries.

I’ve attached our current subprocessor inventory and a draft dataflow PDF (v2) that I pulled together after the demo. Neha — could you please confirm the following by EOD Wednesday?
- Does Redwood ever persist unencrypted input or output to disk outside customer-controlled KMS boundaries? (short answer)
- Which subprocessors, if any, would see decrypted payloads for troubleshooting or pipeline jobs?
- If OrbitCloud (our optional hosting partner in some regions) is in the serving path, do they ever have access to keys or plaintext?

Marissa — can you include a quick paragraph on our standard DPA wording for KMS + key custody (a one-paragraph summary for the legal team to consume)?

I’ll loop OrbitCloud if we need a confirmation from their side.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: redwood_subprocessor_inventory_2027-05.csv (csv, 32KB); edgehealth_dataflow_v2.pdf (pdf, 420KB)

From: Legal <legal@edgehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: security@edgehealth.com
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 11:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Ava — thanks for the materials. A few clarifying points from our side:

- EdgeHealth requires that any vendor serving protected health information (PHI) supports customer-managed keys (CMKs) with no escrow by the vendor, or alternatively a BAA that explicitly documents permitted key usage and emergency access procedures.
- We need to confirm any subprocessors that could access plaintext for troubleshooting, analytics, or model optimization. If a subprocessor can access plaintext, we expect contractual constraints and audit evidence (SOC2 type II evidence + role-based access logs).
- Export-control: we need to know the physical/geo location where decryption can occur. If any decryption event happens in a non-US/EU region, we need to assess export implications.

Please provide: (A) a short yes/no on whether Redwood ever persists plaintext outside CMK-encrypted blobs, (B) the list of subprocessors that could access plaintext and a short description of purpose, (C) the geo footprint for decryption events.

If you have a concise DPA paragraph for key custody, that would help speed internal review.

—
Dana Morales
Sr Counsel, Privacy & Security
EdgeHealth


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: legal@edgehealth.com
Date: Wed, 05 May 2027 08:56:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — draft answers

Forwarding draft answers for review — writing inline for clarity. Ava / Marissa please edit and I'll finalize.

--- Neha (draft) ---
A) Plaintext persistence: No. Redwood does not persist customer inputs or generated outputs in plaintext outside of customer-controlled encryption boundaries. All production stores that may persist request/response KV or logs are encrypted at rest; customer-specific model caches can be configured to use customer-managed KMS keys. Temporary memory-resident plaintext exists in process during inference for low-latency serving but is never written to disk unless explicitly configured for debugging by the customer.

B) Subprocessors with potential plaintext access:
- OrbitCloud (optional hosting partner): may see plaintext only if the customer chooses to deploy with Orbit-managed keys and enables Orbit-managed hosting; if the customer provides CMKs and we configure wrapping so Redwood never exports unwrapped keys, OrbitCloud cannot decrypt payloads. Purpose: optional region hosting/edge routing.
- Support contractors (redacted vendor name): limited access to logs/tooling for debugging under strict role-based access; require beat-the-door escalation and provide auditable access logs.
- Model optimization partner (analytics): does not have access to raw customer payloads under default config; we only share anonymized aggregated telemetry unless customer opts in for sample-level analysis.

C) Geo footprint for decryption events:
- Default hosted path: decryption occurs in the region where the model serving is provisioned (customer-configurable). We can ensure US-only decryption for EdgeHealth by pinning serving to US regions and using CMKs backed in US-only KMS.

I’ll attach a short KMS custody paragraph for Marissa to polish.

—Neha


From: Dana Morales <legal@edgehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: security@edgehealth.com, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Thu, 06 May 2027 09:14:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Thanks Neha — that helps. A couple of follow-ups from security:

1) OrbitCloud: can Orbit provide a written statement that they do not hold unwrapped customer keys and that they will only have access to plaintext when explicitly configured by the customer? If Redwood can facilitate a short attestation from Orbit that would be ideal.
2) Support contractors: we need the vendor name and SOC2 evidence that covers the relevant subprocessors.
3) Can you provide a redline-friendly DPA paragraph for keys and a proposed addendum that we can circulate internally (one paragraph + a one-page appendix listing subprocessors and the exact data flow).

If you can send an initial draft addendum today we can get it into our procurement queue — otherwise we’ll schedule a short follow-up next week.

—Dana


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>
To: Dana Morales <legal@edgehealth.com>, security@edgehealth.com
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>, OrbitCloud Partnerships <cloudvendor@orbitcloud.com>
Date: Thu, 06 May 2027 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Dana — thanks for the clear asks. Quick status update and next steps: 

- OrbitCloud attestation: Looping Mark at OrbitCloud on this thread (Mark Hsu, cloudvendor@orbitcloud.com) — Mark, would you be able to confirm in writing that OrbitCloud does not retain unwrapped CMKs and will only process plaintext when a customer chooses Orbit-managed keys? If you can provide the short attestation by EOD Friday, that would keep timing tight.
- Support contractor & SOC2: Neha will attach the vendor name and the latest SOC2 report extract.
- Draft addendum: Marissa has prepared a short key-custody paragraph and an appendix draft listing subprocessors and per-subprocessor dataflow notes. I’ve uploaded both to the shared Drive link here: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/edgehealth-dataflow-v2 (see files: redwood_subprocessor_inventory_2027-05.csv, addendum_kms_paragraph.docx, edgehealth_dataflow_v2.pdf).

Marissa — can you please: (1) attach a redline-friendly paragraph here, and (2) include a one-sentence note on remedies / audit evidence (SOC2 + access logs) that we can reference in the addendum? We’ll then send the package and the Orbit attestation to Dana.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference

title: SIG / CAIQ kickoff — scheduling windows and evidence roadmap
preview:
From: Naomi Blake <naomi.blake@ironshore.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: procure@ironshore.com
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: SIG/CAIQ kickoff request + preliminary questionnaire

Hi Alex —

Thanks for the quick reply over LinkedIn. We're starting an audit track for a prospective customer (one of our enterprise partners) and they requested a SIG/CAIQ + SOC 2 readiness review of vendors. I've attached the initial questionnaire (Ironshore_SIG_Initial.pdf) — most of it is straightforward but the partner will want to review logging/retention and encryption specifics.

Can we schedule a 45-minute kickoff this week to:
- Walk through the CAIQ highlights and any non-standard controls
- Confirm artifact owners and log sample windows
- Get a timeline for Q&A and evidence delivery (we need artifacts by May 14)

title: Confirming: no model personalization + log sanitization plan
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Johnson <ajohnson@cortextech.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Confirming: no model personalization + log sanitization plan

Hi Alex / Elena,

Thanks again for the call this morning — good to meet the CortexTech team. Quick recap and the commitments we discussed so we can move to contracting: 

- Training-on-customer-data: CortexTech will opt out of any \"model improvement\" or training pipelines. Redwood will not persist any customer data for model retraining or external tuning.
- Real-time inference telemetry: We will collect minimal operational telemetry (inference latency, error rates, token counts).
- PII handling: Any values matching PII patterns (SSNs, credit card numbers, unstructured personal emails) will be removed or replaced via automatic redaction before storage or logs.
- Log retention & anonymization: Logs (request/response) will be stored for 60 days by default, with automatic field-level hashing of user identifiers. After 60 days the records are permanently deleted.
- Data residency: All production inference and logs for your account will live in the US region (us-west-2) unless you request EU residency.

Next steps I promised:
1) I attached our 1-pager on privacy posture and a draft DPA addendum that encodes the opt-out on training and the 60-day retention.
2) Rafael will follow up with a short note on how PII redaction works (regex + entity detector + configurable policy).
3) Lauren, can you confirm legal review timeline for the DPA addendum?

If that matches what you require, I can attach the signed addendum to the SOW and hand to procurement. If anything above is incorrect or you'd like the retention shortened to 14 days we can update the DPA.

Best,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0192

Attachments: redwood_privacy_onepager.pdf, DPA_Addendum_v1.docx


From: Elena Morales <elena.morales@cortextech.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Johnson <ajohnson@cortextech.com>, Procurement <procurement@cortextech.com>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Confirming: no model personalization + log sanitization plan

Aditya —

Thanks for the summary, that looks broadly correct and appreciated for the clarity. A few quick clarifications from our side: 

1) Auditability: Our security team needs immutable audit logs showing access to raw request data (even if redacted) for 180 days as part of our audit program. Is there a mechanism to export redaction proofs or access logs that meet SOC2/AICPA standards?
2) Tokenization: Can you confirm whether the KV cache used for latency improvements ever stores unredacted user content? We need guarantees that the cache also enforces the same PII redaction and TTLs.
3) Procurement asked about liability language — they'll want the opt-out on model training codified in the DPA and a simple addendum to the main SaaS agreement.

Alex and I prefer the 60-day retention if redaction proofs are available; otherwise we may ask for 14 days. Please have Rafael and Lauren weigh in so we can confirm for procurement.

Thanks,
Elena Morales
Head of Platform Security | CortexTech
elena.morales@cortextech.com


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@cortextech.com>, Alex Johnson <ajohnson@cortextech.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>

Subject: Re: Confirming: no model personalization + log sanitization plan

Hi Elena / Alex — quick technical replies inline: 

1) Auditability: Redwood can emit an export package with redaction checksums and an access audit trail that shows which internal Redwood users accessed the tenant logs, timestamps, and operation IDs. That package is stored for 180 days upon request (export only) and is signed with our internal artifact signature. We do not keep the raw unredacted payload after the configured retention window.

2) KV cache: The inference KV cache is configured per-tenant. We do not write raw unredacted customer text to disk in the cache; all KV entries are derived from tokenized, normalized representations. For customers that opt out of any model learning, we disable cross-tenant cache sharing and enforce a per-tenant TTL that defaults to 1 hour (configurable down to 0). We will include this configuration in the DPA addendum.

3) DPA language: Aditya — suggest adding a paragraph explicitly stating: \"Redwood will not use customer-provided data to improve models or models' parameters. All telemetry and logs will be anonymized prior to any persistent storage. Customer may request export of signed redaction proofs for up to 180 days following data creation.\"

If the CortexTech security team wants a short architecture diagram I can send a one-pager showing where redaction runs in the pipeline.

Hi Alex / Elena,

Thanks again for the call this morning — good to meet the CortexTech team. Quick recap and the commitments we discussed so we can move to contracting: 

- Training-on-customer-data: CortexTech will opt out of any \"model improvement\" or training pipelines. Redwood will not persist any customer data for model retraining or external tuning.
- Real-time inference telemetry: We will collect minimal operational telemetry (inference latency, error rates, token counts).
- PII handling: Any values matching PII patterns (SSNs, credit card numbers, unstructured personal emails) will be removed or replaced via automatic redaction before storage or logs.
- Log retention & anonymization: Logs (request/response) will be stored for 60 days by default, with automatic field-level hashing of user identifiers. After 60 days the records are permanently deleted.
- Data residency: All production inference and logs for your account will live in the US region (us-west-2) unless you request EU residency.

Next steps I promised:
1) I attached our 1-pager on privacy posture and a draft DPA addendum that encodes the opt-out on training and the 60-day retention.

title: Post-demo: recommended shortlist + pilot readiness for NovaHealth
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lena Ortiz <lena.ortiz@novahealth.com>, Rajiv Menon <rajiv.menon@novahealth.com>
Cc: 
Date: Wed, 05 May 2027 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo recap + evaluation-ready shortlist and pilot plan

Thanks again for taking the call this morning — great discussion and very helpful signals about the clinical summarization use case and throughput expectations. Below is a concise recap, our recommended shortlist with the tradeoffs we think matter most for NovaHealth, and proposed next steps for a 2-week evaluation.

Quick recap
- Goal: real-time clinical note summarization and SNOMED tagging for intake notes; 90th percentile latency target <= 350ms per inference with burst capacity for peak clinic hours.
- Quality priorities: reduce hallucinations for medication fields, maintain structured tag accuracy > 92% on our test prompt set.
- Constraints: PHI-in-scope for pilot (red flag for hosted logs), preference for VPC/private deployment for production.

Recommended shortlist (pilot-ready) — rationale (cost / latency / quality)
1) redwood-medium-v2 (quantized)
   - Quality: Strong for instruction-following and structured outputs; lowest hallucination rate on our internal meds testset.
   - Latency: ~180–240ms p95 on our 80-token baseline on the dedicated config.
   - Cost: mid-range; good compute/quality balance.
2) llama3-7B-q4f (optimized kernel)
   - Quality: Good for short-form summarization; marginally worse on entity recall but faster decode.
   - Latency: ~120–200ms p95.
   - Cost: lower unit cost, useful as a fallback during peak load.
3) openopt-13B-pt (pruned + 8-bit)
   - Quality: Highest ROUGE / structured tag scores in our benchmark for longer notes but with higher compute.
   - Latency: ~300–400ms p95 depending on sequence length.
   - Cost: premium; recommended only if quality delta justifies.

Why this slate?
- We want a primary model that meets your quality constraints with headroom (redwood-medium-v2) and a lower-cost, lower-latency fallback (llama3-7B-q4f). openopt-13B is an option if you prioritize recall on longer notes and accept higher cost.

Artifacts attached / shared links
- Prompt sets and eval scripts: nova_prompt_sets_v1.zip (attached)
- Benchmark summary (csv): benchmark_summary_nova_20270505.csv (attached)
- SOC2 extract + deployment notes: SOC2-extract-redwood.pdf (attached)
- Live report: https://s3.redwood-inference.com/nova/benchmarks/summary-20270505.html

Pilot proposal (2-week window)
- Week 0 (quick start): SSO + minimal VPC access, seed 5k notes for offline eval (we can accept sanitized export).
- Week 1: Run parallel scoring across shortlist; collect latency/cost metrics and a 1k-sample QA sweep.
- Week 2: Tune prompt templates and select final candidate for a small shadow deployment.

Asks / next steps
- Can you confirm (a) whether we should treat the pilot data as PHI (requires VPC) and (b) two engineers who will be point-of-contact for infrastructure access?
- If you’re comfortable, I’ll loop in Karthik to scope the pilot infra and Marissa on CS ops to own evaluation logistics.

Thanks — happy to sync for 20 minutes to walk through the benchmark table.

—
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
+1 (415) 555-0133

Attachments: nova_prompt_sets_v1.zip, benchmark_summary_nova_20270505.csv, SOC2-extract-redwood.pdf

2) Hallucination metrics: in your benchmark csv I see "medication hallucination rate" called out. Can you surface the exact prompt(s) used for that test (we want to validate with our internal clinicians)?

3) Pricing floor: curious if you can share an estimate for the two-model path (redwood-medium-v2 primary + llama3-7B-q4f fallback) for an expected monthly throughput of ~12M tokens. We can treat that as a placeholder for internal procurement.

Also looping Rajiv (prod infra) — Rajiv, can you share timing for providing an S3 export of 5k sanitized notes?

Thanks — Lena

Lena — great questions. Short answers below; detailed notes and artifacts are attached and I've asked Karthik to weigh in on the infra specifics.

1) VPC for pilot: we can run the 2-week pilot in a customer-isolated VPC with minimal setup. It is not gated behind the full enterprise contract — we support evaluation VPCs for pilots with a short onboarding checklist (SSO + OAuth token exchange + agreed retention). See page 2 of SOC2-extract-redwood.pdf for data handling.

2) Hallucination prompts: attached in nova_prompt_sets_v1.zip under /eval/medication_hallucination_prompt.txt. The prompt explicitly requests structured JSON with medication name, dose, route; we then run an entity-level match to the ground truth to compute hallucination rate. Happy to walk your clinicians through the prompt logic.

3) Pricing estimate (monthly, rough) for 12M tokens
- redwood-medium-v2 (primary): $2,900–$3,400/mo (based on dedicated batch config)
- llama3-7B-q4f (fallback): $1,100–$1,400/mo (used for 20–30% of traffic as fallback)
- Estimated combined blended run-rate: ~$3,300–$4,000/mo (excludes one-time pilot engineering onboarding fee)

Pilot logistics / asks
- Rajiv: please share S3 export path and the sanitized sample by EOD Friday so we can seed the offline eval. If you need a scrub checklist, Marissa will share our recommended PII removal template.
- Karthik / Marissa: can you confirm availability for a 30-minute kickoff Tuesday morning PT? Karthik, can you also confirm estimated time to stand up the evaluation VPC (approx 2–3 days once we have minimal creds)?

title: Renewal discussion: temporal billing smoothing & commit restructure
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira@polarishealth.com>; Raj Patel <raj.p@polarishealth.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Renewal discussion: temporal billing smoothing & commit restructure

Hi Samira / Raj —

Thanks for the note last week about seasonality in transcription volume. We ran the usage profile with the ops team and put together a renewal draft that tries to balance a stable committed rate with a short-term smoothing buffer for the anticipated summer spike.

Attachment: Polaris_Renewal_Proposal_v1.pdf (proposal: commit tiers, burst buffer, conditional crediting)

High level:
- 12-month term with a quarterly true-up.
- Base commit reduced 15% in Q1 to reflect lower baseline usage, with a temporary burst pool for Q2–Q3 sized to your forecast.
- If actual monthly usage exceeds burst pool by >20% for two consecutive months, automatic overage pricing applies (standard overage rate) — we included a one-time mitigated credit for the first month of breach.

Questions for you:
1) Can Procurement confirm whether a single PO for the 12-month invoice cadence is preferred?
2) Would a one-month SLA credit be acceptable if we miss the 99th percentile latency target during your go-live spike window?

title: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — playbook and ask
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jenna Li <jenna.li@theratech.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2027-05-04T10:12:00-07:00
Subject: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — playbook and ask

Hi Jenna —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our discussion you wanted a short playbook that contrasts (A) fine-grained seat licensing with burst credits vs (B) a capacity-first reserved model with autoscale/burst. I attached a worksheet (redwood-pricing-worksheet.pdf) and the usage breakdown you sent (TheraTech_usage_breakdown.xlsx) so we can keep everything in one place.

High-level questions for you so we design the right commercial:
- What is the steady-state concurrent seats you want guaranteed?
- How predictable are the spikes (same day/time windows vs ad hoc)?
- Do you require per-token cost visibility or is a per-seat + burst model acceptable?

A couple of notes up-front:
- We can do seat blocks (10-seat increments) with automatic bursting that draws from a consumable credits bucket.
- Alternatively, Dedicated reserved capacity with autoscale gives deterministic latency during spikes but requires a larger commit.

If you can confirm the steady-state and an example recent spike (start/end, peak concurrency), I’ll pull together two commercial sketches (12m commit) and an experimental pilot option for three months.

Best,
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachment: redwood-pricing-worksheet.pdf (pricing worksheet, 3 pages)
Attachment: TheraTech_usage_breakdown.xlsx (CSV summary sheet)


From: Jenna Li <jenna.li@theratech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Michael Sanders <michael.sanders@theratech.com>, procurement@theratech.com
Date: 2027-05-04T13:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — playbook and ask

Thanks Ben —

Appreciate the summary and the worksheet. Quick answers below to help you sketch options: 

- Steady-state: ~50 concurrent seats daytime for interactive product usage (US customers).
- Spikes: Nightly batch processing plus marketing experiments. Typical spikes: 400–600 concurrent for a 4–6 hour window, usually overnight PST, 3–4 nights/week. Spikes are somewhat predictable (we can schedule most), but we occasionally have ad-hoc bursts during launches.
- Cost visibility: finance prefers a clear per-token or per-seat equivalent. A hybrid where we see baseline seat cost + consumed burst credits is fine.

Other constraints:
- Procurement will push for a predictable monthly invoice for forecasting.
- Security: we need SOC2 evidence before pilot sign-off (happy to share contacts).

If you can send two price sketches (seat-block baseline + burst credits) and a capacity-reserved sketch, we’ll review with procurement and circle back.

Best,
Jenna
Director, Platform Engineering
TheraTech

> > Thanks again for the call...


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.ai>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-04T15:30:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — need a recommended playbook for TheraTech

Folks —

Forwarding Jenna’s reply (below). TheraTech is at a steady ~50 concurrent but spikes to ~500 for ~5 hours several nights/week. Procurement wants predictable monthly forecasting, but engineering is ok with hybrid visibility. They also asked for SOC2 evidence before pilot sign-off (I already gave them our SOC2 contact page).

Please recommend a concise commercial path and any product cautions. My thinking:
- Option A (preferred by PMs I spoke to): Reserve 100 seats (covers growth and some bursts) + a burst credits bucket that auto-expends when concurrency > reserved. Discount: 15% off list for 12m commit.
- Option B (latency-critical): Dedicated reserved capacity sized to peak (500) with autoscale headroom; higher commit but guaranteed tail latency. Discount: 22% with 12m (or 28% for 24m).
- Option C (metered): Pay-as-you-go per token with a monthly committed spend (no seats). Easier forecast for finance if we translate to an equivalent per-seat cost.

Questions for you:
- Is 100-seat baseline defensible or should we recommend 150 to reduce burst draw?
- How aggressive should we be on the first-year discount?
- Any compliance red lines we need to flag to procurement?

I’ll pull together two quick sketches once we align.

Thanks,
Ben

--Forwarded message--
From: Jenna Li <jenna.li@theratech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-04 13:05
Subject: Re: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — playbook and ask

[original text omitted]


From: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-05T09:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Seat granularity vs capacity bursting — need a recommended playbook for TheraTech

Ben / Team —

Thanks for the context. My read: TheraTech wants predictable spend for finance but also wants to avoid over-committing for infrequent spikes. Recommend the following playbook (concise):

1) Pilot (90 days) — low-friction proof:
- Baseline reserved: 80 seats (covers steady-state + growth buffer).
- Burst credits: pre-purchased bucket for 2,000 concurrent-hours (applies when concurrency > reserved).
- Metering: token-level logs + per-burst reporting for finance visibility.
- Discount: pilot pricing = 12% off list (no long-term commit).

2) Production proposal (12 months) — preferred commercial:
- Baseline reserved: 120 seats (we recommend 120 to reduce credit consumption and lower effective per-burst unit cost).
- Burst mechanics: elastic draw from credits bucket with automatic top-up alerts and optional hard cap.
- Discount band: 18–20% for 12m commit, 25% for 24m.
- SLA: 99.9% uptime; dedicated routing for tail-latency critical endpoints at an incremental fee.

3) Dedicated option (if they want strong tail-latency guarantees):
- Reserved Dedicated pool sized to their 95th percentile peak (≈450–500).

title: Feature-level margin & CAC calibration — next steps
preview:
From: Hannah Levi <hannah@carewell.health>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 3 May 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow-up on inference economics

Hi Camila —

Thanks again for the call today. Michael and I sketched high-level usage by feature and I attached the spreadsheet (carewell_feature_estimates.xlsx). A few clarifying points we promised to surface: 

- Expected steady-state volume: ~6M tokens/month initially, with spike weeks up to ~12M for seasonal campaigns.
- Feature mix: primary chat assistant ~60% of tokens, document summarization ~25%, embeddings for search ~15%.
- Avg token per chat session (user-visible): ~1.6k tokens; summarization ~800 tokens/call; embedding calls small (~512).
- Target price band: our finance team is targeting list pricing in the $900–$1,400 per 1M tokens range (they gave a hard FY constraint on cost per active feature).
- CAC sensitivity: VP Growth wants 6–9 month payback on feature-level CAC for this pilot cohort.

Can you confirm whether the target range above is something Redwood can model against, and whether you can give a feature-level $/feature estimate (revenue and inferred inference cost) so we can run it through our internal LTV/CAC model?

Also, we may need an anonymized sample invoice or pilot SOW to run through procurement — is that something you can share?

Best,
Hannah
Hannah Levi
Head of Product Operations, CareWell Health

---
Attachment: carewell_feature_estimates.xlsx (spreadsheet, 42KB)

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Hannah Levi <hannah@carewell.health>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Feature-level margin & CAC calibration — next steps

Hannah —

Great notes and thanks for the spreadsheet. Quick recap from our side and an initial set of back-of-envelope numbers to validate against your finance assumptions. I called out assumptions explicitly so you can swap them into your model.

Assumptions (configurable):
- Suggested model family: mid-sized open model (quantized) — inference cost we use for baseline: $90 per 1M tokens (this reflects batching, KV-cache, and mixed-precision execution in a dedicated-pool scenario).
- Target list price range (your ask): $900–$1,400 per 1M tokens.
- Avg tokens by feature (from your sheet): chat 1,600; summarization 800; embedding 512.
- Expected steady usage: 6M tokens / month.

Initial economics (baseline):
- Internal inference cost per token = $90 / 1,000,000 = $0.00009
- Cost per chat session = 1,600 * $0.00009 = $0.144 (14.4¢)
- Cost per summarization = 800 * $0.00009 = $0.072 (7.2¢)
- Cost per embedding = 512 * $0.00009 = $0.046 (4.6¢)

If you price at $1,200 per 1M tokens (midpoint of your band):
- Revenue per token = $1,200 / 1,000,000 = $0.0012
- Revenue per chat = 1,600 * $0.0012 = $1.92
- Gross margin per chat session = (1.92 - 0.144) / 1.92 ≈ 92.5%
- Revenue per summarization = 800 * $0.0012 = $0.96; margin ≈ 92.5%

CAC / Payback illustration (very rough):
- If an average new customer cohort (pilot) costs $40,000 to acquire (sales + onboarding + incentives), and expected initial MRR from this cohort = (6M tokens / month) * ($1,200 / 1M) = $7,200/month, then simple CAC payback ≈ 40,000 / 7,200 ≈ 5.6 months.

Notes and levers we can use to improve margins or payback:
- Move chat model to a cheaper quantized variant for non-L7 responses (saves ~20–40% on inference cost at minimal quality impact for many prompts).
- Apply prefix caching for repeat flows (CareWell has many repeated structured prompts in triage) — can cut token consumption by up to 25% on those flows.
- Commit to a 6–12 month reserved contract for a discounted list price (we can extend a committed discount that improves CAC payback materially).

As next steps we recommend:
1) Confirm which list price within the $900–$1,400 band you want modeled ($/1M token).
2) Confirm commitment length (month-to-month pilot vs 6–12 month reserved).
3) Approve Ben to run a short pilot config (batch sizes, cache rules, quantization) on a subset of traffic to get an empirical inference-cost number (will produce a 2–3 page pilot SOW).

Ben — can you draft the pilot configuration and a one-page costing summary we can hand to CareWell procurement?

Hannah — happy to jump on 30m tomorrow if that helps speed approvals.

Thanks,
Camila
Camila Reyes
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: draft-mmp-ma-costing.pdf (SOW stub, 12KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Hannah Levi <hannah@carewell.health>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Feature-level margin & CAC calibration — next steps

Adding a bit more granularity to the pilot costing and the empirical levers Camila mentioned. Quick bullets: 

- Pilot config (recommended): 2-week run, mirror 10% of production traffic (chat + summarization mix), use quantized LLM variant + 1 prefix-cache rule set. Expected measured baseline cost reduction vs container baseline: ~28–35%.
- Measurement outputs: tokens consumed, average latency, per-token cost after batching, cache hit rate, and a per-feature cost CSV.
- Deliverable: one-page SOW + measured cost CSV + summary deck.

Example per-feature run-rate (if pilot confirms 30% cost reduction):
- Adjusted inference cost per 1M tokens = $90 * 0.7 = $63 / 1M tokens.
- Adjusted cost per chat = 1,600 * $0.000063 = $0.1008 (10.1¢)
- With $1,200 / 1M tokens pricing, margin per chat increases to (1.92 - 0.1008) / 1.92 ≈ 94.8%

I'll draft the SOW and the one-page costing and share by EOD. Camila — do you want me to include a line item for a 10% pilot setup credit (we typically offer this for strategic pilots)?

Ben
Sr Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: pilot-sow-outline.docx (outline, 8KB)

To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Michael Cho <michael.cho@carewell.health>
Date: Wed, 5 May 2027 11:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Feature-level margin & CAC calibration — next steps

Thanks Camila and Ben — this is exactly what we needed. A couple of quick replies: 

- CareWell finance is comfortable modeling at $1,200 / 1M tokens to start.
- We can commit to a 6 month reserved pilot with a defined traffic floor (we'll confirm the floor in the next 48h after re-checking forecasts).
- CAC budget note: we can allocate a maximum of $35k for this pilot cohort if the SOW includes a clear measurement plan and a 30%+ expected cost reduction target.

Please go ahead and send the one-pager + SOW. Michael and I will review and route to procurement. If you can include an estimated timeline for pilot kickoff (we're aiming for mid-June), that would be great.

Talk soon —
Hannah

From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>

Ben — I dug into the us-west spike overnight. Quick findings:

- The 5/1 09:00 UTC p95 jump aligns with a change in kernel fallback routing we rolled out for experimental Llama shard packing (see change-id deploy-20270501-llama).
- Initial sampling shows request mix shifted to longer sequences for ~4% of requests from a customer subset (includes Acme).
- No single-host OOMs or hardware errors — the increase looks algorithmic (kernel choice + sequence mix).

Recommendation: escalate to P1 for QBR narrative (post-mortem + root cause) but treat mitigation as a fast experiment set: revert fallback for 24h window on a small percentage of traffic, and enable tighter KV cache reuse for long sequences.

Irene

Redwood Inference

title: Re: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning
preview:
From: procurement@novuscorp.com
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning

Ben —

Thanks for the quick intro last week. Attached is our RFI for model catalog governance and licensing (we focused a few sections on open-model provenance, license compatibility, and version pinning/update notifications). Can you confirm by EOD Monday whether Redwood can provide the level of artifact metadata and formal attestation we list (SBOMs, license manifests, canonical checksums), and whether you offer a contractable pinning SLA for hosted models?

We may need a short tech call to walk through one of your model entries.

Thanks —
Emily Stone
Senior Procurement Manager
Novus Corp
procurement@novuscorp.com

Attachment: Novus_RFI_Model_Catalog_2027-05-02.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Stone <emily.stone@novuscorp.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning

Emily —

Appreciate the RFI. I'm attaching our high-level responses and two supporting artifacts: a license attestation and an initial model-catalog extract. High-level answers up-front:

- We publish machine-readable provenance metadata for all catalog models (model identifier, source checkpoint hash, training dataset provenance summary, declared license).
- For open-source models we track the original upstream license (Apache-2.0, MIT, GPL variants, etc.) and maintain an internal compatibility registry.
- We provide immutable model identifiers (model_name@sha256) for all served variants; for customers on Dedicated or Private we support long-lived version pins.
- Hosted customers may request a pinned hosted variant for contractual periods; standard hosted flow supports pin-to-minor for up to 90 days and an enterprise pinning add-on for longer commitments.
- We commit to coordinated update notifications: 30 days for major changes that may affect API compatibility, 7 days for security patches that could change behavior.

I want to validate a couple of Novus-specific asks before firming language for legal: 1) Do you require a signed attestation per model or a single umbrella attestation? 2) Are you asking for automated push notifications (webhook) or email + console notice?

I can kick off a focused technical session next week — proposed slots Tue/Wed afternoon PST. Karthik, can you join to confirm our contractable pin options?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0219

Attachments: Redwood_Model_Catalog_RFI_v1.pdf (application/pdf); License_Attestation_2027-05-02.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Emily Stone <emily.stone@novuscorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T14:26:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning

Hi Ben —

Thanks — quick clarifications from our legal team:
1) We prefer a per-model manifest (machine-readable) that includes the canonical checksum and license at time of serving.
2) For hosted models we need to know whether we can require model pinning in SOWs: if Redwood cannot provide hard pin windows for hosted, we would like an automated rollback/fallback guarantee when a breaking update is deployed.
3) Licensing: if a model is derived from multiple sources with different licenses, we need explicit attribution mapping and the ability to exclude certain upstream weights from being used in our tenant.

Can your provenance metadata include upstream source URIs and a flattened license attribution list? Also — how do you treat models that change license over time (upstream relicensing)?

Emily


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-03T08:47:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning — Novus follow-ups

Team —

Looping you in on a Novus RFI. They want per-model machine-readable manifests (checksum, license, upstream URIs), contractual pinning options for hosted, and clear handling of relicensing. Specific asks inlined below. Can you confirm practical responses we can put in an SOW?

Key questions for answers:
- Can we provide per-model manifests with immutable model_name@sha256 and include upstream source URIs?
- For hosted customers: what pinning windows are doable without creating operational fragility? Can we offer an enterprise pin that guarantees a pinned hosted variant for N months? What rollback/fallback guarantees can we give?
- How do we handle upstream relicensing changes — can we surface alerts and provide migration guidance or rollback to last compatible pinned internal variant?

Neha — can you attach the model_provenance_schema and suggested wording for the manifest? Karthik — can you confirm commercial options (pinning add-on, notification SLA) and the wording legal wants?

Ben


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-03T11:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning — Novus follow-ups

Ben / Karthik —

See notes and suggested SOW/manifest language below (I also attached a simplified schema and examples). Summary:

Provenance & manifests:
- We provide a machine-readable manifest per served model variant: fields include model_id (human name), model_digest (sha256 of packaged weights), source_uri (list of upstream checkpoints or repos), training_data_summary (high-level dataset provenance), declared_license (SPDX id), license_attribution_list (flattened list of upstream authors/weights).
- model_id format: <catalog_name>@sha256:<digest> (e.g., redwood/gpt-small@sha256:3f2a...). This ID is immutable once published to the catalog and attached to audit logs.
- We will keep a historical snapshot of manifests in an immutable store for 5 years (audit trail) and provide exports on request.

Pinning & hosted options:
- Dedicated/Private: full support for long-lived pinning (customer controlled), can pin indefinitely subject to contract and capacity.
- Hosted: we can offer two commercial pinning modes:
  1) Standard hosted pin-to-minor: pin a hosted endpoint to a minor version for up to 90 days (no additional fee).
  2) Enterprise hosted pin: contract add-on that guarantees a pinned hosted variant for a negotiated term (90–720 days). This is backed by configuration locks and a capacity reservation; pricing and lead time apply.
- Rollback/fallback: hosted endpoints support automatic fallback policies — if a new default model variant fails health checks for a tenant, traffic can be routed to the last pinned variant for that tenant for up to 14 days while we remediate. Enterprise pin ensures longer fallback windows.

Relicensing & attribution changes:
- We monitor upstream license changes for tracked sources. If an upstream relicenses in a way that affects compatibility, we will:
  a) Flag impacted catalog entries and surface notifications to the tenant (console + email).
  b) Provide the last compatible pinned variant and guidance for remediating.
  c) For policy-violating relicenses (e.g., license becomes incompatible with customer's requirements), we will support a migration plan and, where feasible, maintain a forked/clean checkpoint for customers on Dedicated/Private.
- Manifest includes source_uri and checksum so customers can independently verify.

Notifications & delivery:
- Notification channels: console alert, email, and webhook (we support all three).
- Notification windows we propose: 30 days for major API/behavior changes, 7 days for security-related behavioral patches. These windows are contractable for Enterprise pin customers.

Suggested SOW language (short):
- "Redwood will provide per-model, machine-readable manifests (model_name@sha256) including source URIs, checksum, and declared license. Manifests will be retained for 5 years and exported on request. For hosted services Redwood offers standard pinning (90-day pin-to-minor) and an Enterprise Pinning Add-on (contracted term up to 720 days) that guarantees a pinned hosted variant. Redwood will provide coordinated change notifications via console, email, and webhook with a default 30-day notice for major updates and 7-day notice for security fixes. In cases of upstream relicensing Redwood will surface affected entries and provide last-compatible pinned artifacts and migration guidance."

I'll attach model_provenance_schema.json and a pinning_options_matrix.xlsx for Karthik to include in the external response.

-Neha
Research & Applied ML
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai

Attachments: model_provenance_schema.json (application/json); pinning_options_matrix.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

Date: 2027-05-02T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning

Emily —

Appreciate the RFI. I'm attaching our high-level responses and two supporting artifacts: a license attestation and an initial model-catalog extract. High-level answers up-front:

- We publish machine-readable provenance metadata for all catalog models (model identifier, source checkpoint hash, training dataset provenance summary, declared license).
- For open-source models we track the original upstream license (Apache-2.0, MIT, GPL variants, etc.) and maintain an internal compatibility registry.
- We provide immutable model identifiers (model_name@sha256) for all served variants; for customers on Dedicated or Private we support long-lived version pins.
- Hosted customers may request a pinned hosted variant for contractual periods; standard hosted flow supports pin-to-minor for up to 90 days and an enterprise pinning add-on for longer commitments.
- We commit to coordinated update notifications: 30 days for major changes that may affect API compatibility, 7 days for security patches that could change behavior.

I want to validate a couple of Novus-specific asks before firming language for legal: 1) Do you require a signed attestation per model or a single umbrella attestation? 2) Are you asking for automated push notifications (webhook) or email + console notice?

I can kick off a focused technical session next week — proposed slots Tue/Wed afternoon PST. Karthik, can you join to confirm our contractable pin options?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

From: Emily Stone <emily.stone@novuscorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T14:26:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Model catalog provenance, licensing, and versioning

Hi Ben —

Thanks — quick clarifications from our legal team:
1) We prefer a per-model manifest (machine-readable) that includes the canonical checksum and license at time of serving.
2) For hosted models we need to know whether we can require model pinning in SOWs: if Redwood cannot provide hard pin windows for hosted, we would like an automated rollback/fallback guarantee when a breaking update is deployed.
3) Licensing: if a model is derived from multiple sources with different licenses, we need explicit attribution mapping and the ability to exclude certain upstream weights from being used in our tenant.

Can your provenance metadata include upstream source URIs and a flattened license attribution list? Also — how do you treat models that change license over time (upstream relicensing)?

Emily

title: Temporary BYOK retention window exception — coordination & product gaps
preview:
From: Andrea Smith <andrea.smith@cardinalsys.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Temporary BYOK retention exception request for SOC2 evidence

Hi Ben,

Hope you’re well — we’re in the middle of a SOC2 readiness window and procurement has flagged a gap in Redwood’s standard BYOK retention behavior for Cardinal Systems. Our external auditors are asking for 30–90 days of retained audit events tied to key usage and KMS wrapping operations (they need exportable evidence for a sample period).

Summary of asks:
- Do you have a supported way to provide per-customer retained audit logs (key wrap/unwrap events) for a defined window?
- If not, can Redwood provide a short-term exception/process to retain and hand back evidence for the period May 1–May 31, 2027?
- Any required paperwork (exceptions form, DPA addendum, or procurement flow) we need to fast-track?

I attached our requirements PDF (cardinal_byok_requirements.pdf) with the auditor checklist. Procurement is pushing for a confirm/no-confirm by EOD today so we can update the audit plan.

Thanks — Andrea
Andrea Smith
Senior Security & Privacy Manager, Cardinal Systems
andrea.smith@cardinalsys.com
Attachment: cardinal_byok_requirements.pdf

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Temporary BYOK retention exception request for SOC2 evidence

Folks — forwarding Cardinal’s ask (see below). TL;DR: they need retained audit evidence for key wrap/unwrap and KMS-related events for a one-month window to satisfy an audit. Procurement wants confirmation today.

Questions I need answered quickly:
- Can we produce an evidence bundle (audit log exports + signed attestation) without changing production retention behavior?
- Is there a safe temporary process (manual export + escrow) that legal/infra are comfortable with, or do we need product work / config changes?
- If a product change is required (per-customer retention override), rough estimate of scope and ETA.

Please reply with a one-paragraph recommendation and an owner. I’ll coordinate with Cardinal on any paperwork.

---------- Forwarded message ----------
From: Andrea Smith <andrea.smith@cardinalsys.com>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Temporary BYOK retention exception request for SOC2 evidence
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

[original message attached]


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 11:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Temporary BYOK retention exception request for SOC2 evidence

Ben / team — quick technical triage below.

Summary of current state:
- Our BYOK flow (customer-managed keys) never stores plaintext keys; we keep cryptographic metadata and non-sensitive operational logs. The default retention for low-level KMS traces is short (7 days) and not intended for audit handback.
- We can export key-operation events (wrap/unwrap, authorization checks, request metadata) from the runtime logs, but that requires a manual extraction from the ops pipeline.

Recommendation (short-term, low-risk):
1) Manual evidence bundle: ops run an export for the requested date range (May 1–31) including key-operation events and correlated request IDs. We can redact any unrelated PII and provide a signed attestation describing what was extracted. Delivery: 3 business days if we prioritize.
2) Product path (medium-term): add a per-customer retention override + audit-exports feature. This is non-trivial: touches storage retention config, billing, and privacy controls; estimate 4–6 sprints with 2 engineers + infra work. Requires legal/contract review on long-term retention.
3) If procurement requires a formal exception for policy, we should pair the manual export with a short (30–90 day) documented exception and a DocuSign acceptance.

Action items / owners:
- Evidence export (ops): assign to Marcus (3 business days) — includes export + simple attestation template.
- Legal/Privacy: Kimberly to advise on wording and whether we can sign a short exception or need DPA addendum.
- Product: I’ll put a scoped ticket for per-customer retention and drop a note to roadmap — not blocking the audit but required for future cases.

I’ll post the SEC ticket after this thread and link it here.

— Jonas
Jonas Weber
Head of Security Engineering, Redwood Inference
Attachment: evidence_export_template.txt

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Andrea Smith <andrea.smith@cardinalsys.com>, procurement@cardinalsys.com
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 May 2027 13:38:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Temporary BYOK retention exception request for SOC2 evidence

Andrea — thanks for the fast heads-up and the PDF. Internal triage complete; here’s the agreed path so we can close this for procurement today.

What Redwood will do (short-term, agreed):
- Produce an evidence bundle containing exported key-operation events and correlated request-level logs for May 1–31, 2027. The bundle will be redacted for unrelated PII and accompanied by a signed attestation from Redwood Security describing the scope. (Owner: Marcus Lin; ETA: 3 business days.)
- To proceed we need a signed short-term exception: a one-page form confirming Cardinal requests Redwood to extract and retain evidence for the defined window and that Cardinal accepts responsibility for storing that evidence after delivery. We can send via DocuSign.

What is NOT changing today:
- We will not modify global retention policies or introduce per-customer retention toggles in production as an immediate change. Product work to add that capability is being scoped (non-urgent for this ask).

Attachments: proposed_workaround.md (details of the export), evidence_checklist.pdf (what we will hand back).

Next steps for Cardinal:
1) Confirm you accept the short-term manual export + exception path.
2) Provide the preferred contact for DocuSign (person + email) so we can route the exception form.

Thanks — we’ll prioritize the export once we have the signed exception.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
Attachments: proposed_workaround.md, evidence_checklist.pdf

preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>

Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:12:00-07:00
Subject: End-of-commitment notice matrix & redline handoff

Hi Marta / James —

Thanks for the time on yesterday's call. Attached is our current MSA redline and a draft order form for the pilot. Key items we flagged for legal/procurement alignment:

- Auto-renew: Redwood's standard stance is a 60-day opt-out notice before the renewal effective date. We saw Orchid's request for a shorter 45-day window in procurement notes — want to understand whether that's for pilot only or enterprise-wide.
- Termination for convenience: current MSA allows termination with 60 days' notice for enterprise engagements. Orchid's procurement suggested 30 days. We can make a concession for pilot projects but need a clearer wind-down and refund mechanism.
- Transition assistance & data handoff: propose 30 days minimum for transfer support if either party terminates for convenience; costs / pro-rated fees to be clarified in order form.

I've copied Karthik and Laura to confirm our recommended positions; Laura, please call out any must-haves for the MSA language. Marta/James, can you confirm procurement's target notice for pilot vs. production? If you prefer, we can propose a split-window (pilot 30–45 days, enterprise 60).

Attachments: MSA_Redline_v3.docx (redline), OrderForm_Orchid_20270502.xlsx (pricing draft)

Next steps: would like to lock on a compromise by EOD Friday May 6 so legal can produce a consolidated redline and we can send DocuSign.

Best,
Ava Chen
Director, Commercial Partnerships
Redwood Inference

--


From: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@orchid.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, James O'Neill <james.oneill@orchid.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T14:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: End-of-commitment notice matrix & redline handoff

Ava —

Thanks for the note and the redline. Quick clarifications from Orchid procurement:

- Auto-renew: Procurement's preference is a 45-day opt-out across the agreement for predictability. For pilots, we were thinking 30 days, but would accept 45 for consistency.
- Termination for convenience: we need the ability to exit pilots with 30 days' notice and enterprise with 45 days. We are not comfortable with 60 days for enterprise without a stronger service commitment.
- Refunds: we expect pro-rated refunds for prepaid amounts less any actual consumed usage. No early-termination penalty for pilots; for enterprise, a modest wind-down fee is acceptable but should be capped at one month's committed fees.

I've attached a PDF with our marked comments (Orchid_MSA_Comments.pdf). Happy to jump on a short call with your legal team to resolve the pilot vs enterprise split.

Best,
Marta Lopez
Head of Procurement
Orchid Analytics

--


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: internal — positions on notice & termination windows

Ava / Laura —

Summarizing recommended positions before we reply to Orchid:

1) Auto-renew: keep enterprise at 60 days (standard operationally), offer 45 days as a concession only if Orchid commits to a 30% prepayment schedule or a minimum annual commitment. For pilots, agree to 30 or 45 days depending on pilot length.
2) Termination for convenience: accept a tiered model — pilot: 30 days with no early termination fee; enterprise: 60 days notice with either (a) pro-rated refund for unused prepaid amounts minus reasonable wind-down costs, or (b) an election for customer to pay one quarter of the committed remaining fees as a buyout. We should aim to cap any wind-down fee to limit disputes.
3) Transition assistance: codify 30 days of minimal transition assistance with optional paid extended support at our standard T&M rates.

Laura — please review and flag mandatory legal language. I'm attaching a short internal summary so we can track positions (internal-redline-summary.txt).

-Karthik
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

--


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T13:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: internal — legal note on termination/renewal language

Karthik / Ava —

Thanks — quick legal notes on the redline:

- Section 8.2 (Termination for Convenience): propose we use a tiered clause. Wording suggestion: "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon written notice to the other party as follows: (i) for Pilot Agreements, 30 days' prior written notice; (ii) for Enterprise Agreements, 45 days' prior written notice where the Customer has a rolling monthly/quarterly commitment, and 60 days' prior written notice where Customer has annual prepayment or committed capacity." This gives us flexibility operationally.
- Refunds & fees: refunds should be limited to unused prepaid fees less our reasonable wind-down costs (defined as costs incurred to deprovision/custom export, estimated not-to-exceed X% of remaining term). For enterprise buyout, we can agree a capped buyout equal to one month's average monthly committed fees to keep it simple.
- Auto-renew: propose we accept 45 days for enterprise in exchange for a 12-month minimum commitment or minimum spend threshold. Otherwise keep 60 days. For pilots, accept 30 days.
- Transition assistance: require explicit SOW/Order Form to document deliverables and rates for post-termination support. Minimum 30 days' assistance included for all terminations for convenience.

I'll convert these into tracked changes and produce legal-redline-notes.docx; once we have procurement's final ask we should push back with a single clean compromise redline for signature.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

--


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@orchid.ai>, James O'Neill <james.oneill@orchid.ai>

- Auto-renew: Pilot agreements — 30 day opt-out; Enterprise agreements — 60 day opt-out standard. We will accept 45 days for enterprise only if Orchid agrees to a 12-month minimum spend or a 30% prepayment on order execution.
- Termination for convenience: Pilot — 30 days' notice, no termination fee; Enterprise — 45 days' notice if rolling monthly/quarterly commitments, 60 days' notice for annual prepayments/committed capacity. Refunds limited to unused prepaid fees less reasonable wind-down costs; as a simplified option, a capped buyout equal to one month's average committed fees is acceptable.
- Transition assistance: 30 days included; any additional support will be covered at standard T&M rates documented in the SOW.

Attached is a consolidated redline reflecting the language above (MSA_Redline_v4.docx) and a clean order form (OrderForm_Orchid_20270502.xlsx). If this is acceptable, we can push the redline into DocuSign and aim to have countersigned docs within one week.

title: Request: one-off credit forgiveness & sandbox restart for OliveTech POC
preview:
From: Sara Patel <sara@olivetech.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 1 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Trial expired — request for credits to restart POC

Hi Avery,

Quick background: our small engineering team at OliveTech was midway through a feature POC using Redwood last month. The sandbox expired two weeks ago while procurement routed our PO (we hit a weird internal approval hold). The POC is blocked — we need a short burst of credits to finish the eval so we can sign.

Can Redwood grant a one-time restart (enough credit to run the final 10 model runs — roughly 80–120k tokens)? We can sign a short MAP or PO as soon as procurement unblocks, but we need the sandbox reactivated for ~2–3 weeks to complete the tests.

I attached a small usage report (usage_report_may2027.csv) showing the runs left and expected token burn. If there are alternative options (shorter extension + staged restart), we can adapt. Budget is tight — we were close to selecting Redwood for latency/throughput reasons, so any help here is appreciated.

Thanks,
Sara Patel
Product Manager, OliveTech
Attachments: usage_report_may2027.csv (CSV, 12KB)


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Sara Patel <sara@olivetech.ai>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 1 May 2027 09:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Trial expired — request for credits to restart POC

Hi Sara — thanks for the quick note and for the usage attachment. 

Short answer: we can usually do a one-time make-good for pilot customers while procurement finishes. I need to loop in my manager (Neha) and finance (Marcus) for approval on the exact amount. My initial recommendation: 100k tokens + 21 days of sandbox access so your team can finish the tests and sign the MAP/PO. If you think that's not enough, share the expected runs breakdown (you already did — looks like ~80–120k) and a target sign date.

Next steps from our side:
- I will propose a one-time credit (100k) internally and ask for a conditional approval tied to a signed MAP or PO within 30 days.
- Can procurement share a tentative PO ETA? If it's >30 days we may need finance to treat it as a credit with collection terms.

I’ll follow up once Neha/Marcus weigh in.

—Avery
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery_johnson@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0187


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 2 May 2027 08:14:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: OliveTech — trial credit forgiveness request / recommendation

Forwarding OliveTech request — they lost access when their sandbox expired because procurement was delayed. Key facts: customer is a mid-stage startup (seed + Series A MRR), POC is production-relevant (latency+throughput win), likely 1–2 seat expansion after sign.

My recommended posture: grant a one-time 100k-token credit and reopen their sandbox for 21 days. Condition: signed Mutual Action Plan (MAP) or PO within 30 days. Rationale: small short-term cost, high chance of conversion and expansion; preserves sales momentum.

Estimated short-term cost impact: 100k tokens @ typical model mix ~= $75–120 (gross cost) — negligible vs value. If approved, I’ll record deal_id DL-2027-3819 and request a billing hold so finance knows to expect PO/collection.

Attachment: usage_report_may2027.csv (from customer)

—Avery


From: Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 May 2027 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: OliveTech — trial credit forgiveness request / recommendation

Thanks for the context, Avery. I’m comfortable approving a conditional make-good. Approve 100k tokens and a 21-day sandbox reactivation under these conditions:

1) OliveTech signs the attached MAP (olive_MAP_2027.docx) or provides a PO within 30 calendar days of today (May 3, 2027).
2) We log deal_id DL-2027-3819 in HubSpot and set a 30-day billing follow-up task on the account owner.
3) Finance (Marcus) places a billing hold/flag and does not waive invoicing — this is a credit advance contingent on receipt of PO.

Marcus — please confirm you’ve noted the deal and can place a billing hold. Once Marcus confirms, Avery please execute the sandbox reopen and send the MAP/DocuSign link to OliveTech.

—Neha
Neha Kapoor
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
neha_kapoor@redwood.com

Sara — good news. Approved: Redwood will apply a one-time credit of 100,000 tokens and reopen your sandbox for 21 days effective now. Conditions (per our policy):

• MAP or PO signature required within 30 days (by June 2, 2027).
• We’ll log this as deal DL-2027-3819 in our system.
• Finance will place a billing hold — if the PO is not received in 30 days, Redwood reserves the right to invoice the advance credit.

Next steps for you:
1) Click this DocuSign link to review & sign the MAP: https://app.redwood.com/docs/mutual-action-plan/DL-2027-3819

Alex / team —

Quick checklist for an invoice-only pilot (based on current playbook):
1) Pilot cap: <= $15,000 (one-off) — ok to approve without PO if other conditions met.
2) Required evidence before start: signed SOW, W-9, COI (insurance).
3) Payment terms: Net 30; require procurement contact and billing email.
4) Risk controls: tag account as "invoice-only pilot" in ERP; 30-day internal review; no auto-renew without PO.
5) Collections: finance to send reminder at 45 days; if unpaid at 60 days, suspend access.

Notes/Questions:
- Insurance: the COI template must show general liability and cyber coverage >= $1M.
- Currency/Tax: assume USD; if GlyphData requires foreign tax docs, escalate.

If Glyph provides the docs and SOW is within the cap, we can move forward. Marcus, Laura — any objections?

Thanks,

Agree with Priya's checklist. A couple of additions from ops perspective:
- Threshold: recommend we set the operational threshold at $12,500 for a single pilot (to match their ask and leave headroom).

- Billing: require procurement billing contact in the SOW and a valid email to receive invoices; use explicit invoice subject including "Pilot — GlyphData — Redwood" to avoid routing delays.
- Escalation: if invoice not paid within 45 days, ops should suspend the API key; collections to cc account owner and procurement team.

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
